syllabus_desc: Define: indicated altitude
concept: Indicated altitude is the altitude displayed on your altimeter with a particular sub-scale (QNH or QFE) set. It's the raw readout before any corrections for temperature or pressure deviations from ISA. Set Christchurch QNH and the altimeter tells you your height above mean sea level -- that's your indicated altitude. Simple, direct, and the number you'll fly by most of the time.
exam_tip: Indicated altitude = what the altimeter actually reads with a QNH or QFE set. No corrections applied.
key_facts: The altitude shown on the altimeter with a specific pressure sub-scale set
No correction for non-ISA temperature or pressure
Used with QNH to read height above MSL at that QNH
Used with QFE to read height above aerodrome elevation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Define: calibrated altitude
concept: Calibrated altitude is indicated altitude corrected for instrument error and position error (from the static system). In older aircraft handbooks you'll sometimes see this called 'rectified altitude'. In practice for NZ IFR exams it's the step between indicated and true -- you strip out the instrument/position errors but not yet the temperature correction.
exam_tip: Calibrated altitude = indicated altitude corrected for instrument error and position (static) error. One step closer to true altitude.
key_facts: Indicated altitude corrected for instrument error and position error
Removes errors introduced by the pitot-static system placement
Sometimes called rectified altitude in older texts
Does not correct for temperature or pressure deviation from ISA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Define: true altitude
concept: True altitude is your actual height above mean sea level -- the real number. You get there by correcting calibrated altitude for the temperature of the air column below you. On a cold day (air denser than ISA), the altimeter over-reads, so true altitude is lower than indicated. 'High to low, look out below' -- below ISA temperature means the terrain is closer than your altimeter suggests. This matters enormously on non-precision approaches in cold conditions.
exam_tip: True altitude = actual height above MSL. Cold air: true altitude is LESS than indicated (terrain closer). Hot air: true altitude is MORE than indicated. High to low, look out below.
key_facts: The actual geometric height above mean sea level
Derived by correcting calibrated altitude for temperature deviation from ISA
Below ISA temp: true altitude < indicated altitude (under-reading -- terrain is closer)
Above ISA temp: true altitude > indicated altitude (over-reading -- terrain is further)
Critical for obstacle clearance on non-precision approaches in cold weather
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Define: pressure altitude (PA)
concept: Pressure altitude (PA) is the altitude indicated when your altimeter sub-scale is set to 1013.2 hPa (standard pressure). It tells you where you are in the standard atmosphere -- not relative to sea level, but relative to the ISA pressure datum. Flight levels use pressure altitude. RVSM operations, performance charts, and high-altitude operations all reference PA.
exam_tip: Pressure altitude = altimeter reading with 1013.2 hPa set on the sub-scale. Used for flight levels and performance calculations.
key_facts: Altitude indicated with sub-scale set to 1013.2 hPa (standard pressure)
Basis for flight levels (e.g. FL150 = 15,000 ft pressure altitude)
Used in performance calculations (density altitude, true airspeed)
All aircraft above transition altitude use pressure altitude for separation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Define: density altitude (DA)
concept: Density altitude (DA) is pressure altitude corrected for temperature -- it's the altitude in the standard atmosphere that has the same air density as your current conditions. High and hot equals high density altitude, which means your aircraft thinks it's at a much higher altitude than it really is. Engine makes less power, props and wings generate less lift, wheels run longer on the takeoff roll. Density altitude doesn't appear on the altimeter -- you calculate it.
exam_tip: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for temperature. High temperature = high DA. DA is what determines aircraft performance, not indicated altitude.
key_facts: Pressure altitude corrected for non-ISA temperature
High DA: reduced engine power, reduced lift, degraded aircraft performance
ISA temperature at a given pressure altitude gives DA = PA
Hot day at high aerodrome: DA can exceed pressure altitude significantly
Not displayed on any instrument -- must be calculated
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Define: flight level (FL)
concept: A flight level (FL) is an expression of pressure altitude in hundreds of feet, with the altimeter set to 1013.2 hPa. FL150 = 15,000 ft pressure altitude. In New Zealand, IFR aircraft cruise at flight levels above the transition altitude of 13,000 ft. The standard pressure datum ensures that all aircraft above transition are using the same reference, so separation works. Below transition, you use QNH and measure in feet AMSL.
exam_tip: Flight level = pressure altitude in hundreds of feet on 1013.2 hPa. FL150 = 15,000 ft on standard pressure. Used above transition altitude in NZ (13,000 ft).
key_facts: Expressed as pressure altitude / 100 (e.g. FL150 = 15,000 ft on 1013.2 hPa)
All aircraft at same FL use same pressure datum -- provides vertical separation
NZ transition altitude: 13,000 ft AMSL
NZ transition level: FL150 (or FL160 when zone QNH is 980 hPa or below)
FL150 and FL155 must not be used as cruising levels when zone QNH is 980 hPa or less
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Define: transition altitude
concept: Transition altitude (TA) in New Zealand is 13,000 ft AMSL. It's the altitude at or below which vertical position is maintained by reference to QNH. Climbing through 13,000 ft, you set 1013.2 hPa. That's the trigger -- 13,000 ft up, standard pressure in. Below it, you use the local or zone QNH. Think of it as the boundary between the QNH world and the flight-level world.
exam_tip: NZ transition altitude = 13,000 ft AMSL. At or below: use QNH. Climbing through: set 1013.2 hPa.
key_facts: NZ transition altitude = 13,000 ft AMSL
At or below TA: maintain vertical position by reference to QNH
When climbing above 13,000 ft: set altimeter to 1013.2 hPa
Defined in AIP NZ ENR 1.7 and CAR Part 91
Expressed as an altitude (feet AMSL), not as a flight level
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Define: transition layer
concept: The transition layer is the airspace between the transition altitude (13,000 ft) and the transition level (FL150). It's a buffer zone -- you're either going up through it or coming down through it. You can't cruise in the transition layer unless ATC specifically authorises it (or under certain special operations conditions). When descending through FL150, you swap from 1013.2 hPa to the appropriate zone QNH.
exam_tip: Transition layer = between 13,000 ft and FL150 in NZ. Transit only (no cruising unless ATC approves). Descending through FL150: set QNH.
key_facts: Airspace between transition altitude (13,000 ft) and transition level (FL150)
Used for climbing and descending only -- no cruising without ATC authorisation
Exception: special activity operations with block clearance
On descent through FL150: set altimeter to zone or aerodrome QNH
On climb above 13,000 ft: set altimeter to 1013.2 hPa
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Define: transition level
concept: The transition level (TL) in New Zealand is normally FL150. It's the lowest flight level available for cruising above the transition layer. It's expressed as a flight level (pressure altitude) rather than an altitude. When zone QNH drops to 980 hPa or below, FL150 gets too close to 13,000 ft for safe separation -- so the TL steps up to FL160. You need to check the current zone QNH to know which TL applies.
exam_tip: NZ transition level = FL150 normally, rises to FL160 when zone QNH is 980 hPa or less. It's the lowest available FL above the transition layer.
key_facts: NZ transition level = FL150 (standard conditions)
When zone QNH is 980 hPa or less: transition level increases to FL160
FL150 and FL155 cannot be used as cruising levels when zone QNH is 980 hPa or less
Expressed as a flight level (on 1013.2 hPa), not as an altitude
Lowest available cruising FL above the transition layer
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Apply the table of IFR cruising levels below and above transition
concept: IFR cruising levels in New Zealand are governed by the semi-circular rule in Table ENR 1.7-2. The direction of track determines which levels are available. Tracks 270 to 089 degrees magnetic: IFR aircraft use odd-thousand FLs (FL150, FL170, FL190 etc.) or odd-thousand-foot altitudes below transition. Tracks 090 to 269 degrees magnetic: even-thousand FLs (FL160, FL180, FL200 etc.) and even-thousand-foot altitudes. Remember FL155 and FL150 are excluded when zone QNH is 980 hPa or below. This table applies in the NZ FIR -- the Auckland Oceanic FIR uses a different table (ENR 1.7-1).
exam_tip: NZ FIR IFR cruising levels: 270-089 MAG = odd-thousand FLs above transition (FL150, FL170...) or odd altitudes below. 090-269 MAG = even-thousand FLs (FL160, FL180...) or even altitudes. Memorise both tables.
key_facts: NZ FIR Table ENR 1.7-2 specifies IFR cruising levels
Tracks 270-089 MAG: FL150, FL170, FL190, FL210, FL230... (odd thousands above transition)
Tracks 090-269 MAG: FL160, FL180, FL200, FL220, FL240... (even thousands above transition)
Below transition: odd/even thousand feet AMSL corresponding to track direction
FL150 and FL155 unavailable as cruising levels when zone QNH is 980 hPa or less
Auckland Oceanic FIR uses separate table (ENR 1.7-1) with different structure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7, CAR 91.425

syllabus_desc: Explain how true and indicated altitudes are affected by changes in air pressure and air temperature
concept: Your altimeter measures atmospheric pressure and converts that to altitude using the ISA model. When the actual atmosphere departs from ISA, indicated altitude diverges from true altitude. Low pressure (below standard): you're lower than the altimeter says -- 'high to low, look out below'. Low temperature: the air is denser and columns are shorter than ISA assumes, so again you're lower than the altimeter shows. High pressure or high temperature: the reverse. For NZ IFR ops, both effects can stack -- a cold, low-pressure day puts you significantly below your indicated altitude.
exam_tip: Low pressure OR low temperature: true altitude < indicated. High pressure OR high temperature: true altitude > indicated. 'High to low, look out below.'
key_facts: Altimeter assumes ISA conditions -- deviations cause errors
Low QNH (pressure below standard): indicated reads high, true altitude is lower
Low temperature (below ISA): air denser, true altitude less than indicated
High QNH: indicated reads low, true altitude is higher than indicated
High temperature: true altitude greater than indicated
Effects add: cold and low-pressure day = largest under-reading of true altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Explain how changes in air pressure and air temperature affect the vertical profile during a non-ILS approach
concept: On a non-ILS approach (VOR, NDB, GNSS), the minimum altitudes (MDA/MDH) are designed for ISA conditions. When temperature is cold, the actual height above terrain at the MDA is less than the altimeter shows -- the air is more compressed, steps in the atmosphere are smaller. So flying a VORDME approach into Queenstown on a cold winter day, your 2,600 ft MDA might put you closer to the terrain than the chart assumes. Similarly, low QNH means the pressure is lower than standard, so you're physically lower than the altimeter indicates. Both effects reduce your actual terrain clearance below the nominal value.
exam_tip: Cold temperature and/or low QNH on a non-ILS approach: actual height above terrain is LESS than indicated. Obstacle clearance is reduced. Cold = closer to the ground than you think.
key_facts: Non-ILS (non-precision) approaches: MDA based on ISA and standard QNH
Cold air: atmosphere compressed, true altitude less than indicated at each step of the approach
Low QNH: aircraft is physically lower than altimeter indicates
Both effects reduce actual terrain clearance below the charted minimum
ILS glideslope unaffected (physical beam guidance) -- non-precision approaches are more vulnerable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Explain the risks associated by low temperatures and QNH errors during a non-ILS approach
concept: The two main risks on a cold, low-QNH non-ILS approach are controlled flight into terrain (CFIT) and descending below MDA with inadequate obstacle clearance. If the QNH is incorrect or outdated, the altimeter error compounds with temperature error -- you could be well below the altitude you think you're at. Some operators apply temperature corrections to all altitude constraints below the transition altitude (following ICAO cold weather correction tables). In New Zealand, CAANZ recommends applying corrections when the outside temperature is significantly below ISA. Not doing so -- especially in places like Queenstown or Milford Sound -- is the kind of oversight that ends careers.
exam_tip: Key risks: CFIT from reduced true terrain clearance, and error compounding if QNH is stale or wrong. Temperature correction tables exist and should be applied in cold conditions on non-precision approaches.
key_facts: Primary risk: CFIT -- terrain closer than altimeter indicates
QNH error and temperature error are cumulative
Stale or incorrect QNH amplifies the temperature effect
Temperature correction tables (ICAO cold weather corrections) exist for non-precision approaches
Most critical at low altitudes (final approach, MDA) and near high terrain
Queenstown, Milford and similar high-terrain NZ airports particularly vulnerable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Explain how true and indicated altitudes are related when using flight levels
concept: When you're flying at a flight level (above transition altitude), both you and every other aircraft are using 1013.2 hPa. Indicated altitude (on standard pressure) equals pressure altitude. True altitude is different -- because actual temperature and pressure deviate from ISA. The altimeter doesn't know the difference between a standard day and a cold one at FL180. So at FL180 in cold air, you're physically lower than 18,000 ft MSL, but you're separated from other FL180 aircraft because they're all on the same pressure datum. Vertical separation is maintained in the FL world by using a common datum, not by knowing true altitude.
exam_tip: At flight levels: indicated = pressure altitude (1013.2 hPa). True altitude differs from pressure altitude due to temperature. Separation is maintained via common pressure datum, not true altitude.
key_facts: Above transition: all aircraft use 1013.2 hPa -- indicated = pressure altitude
True altitude at a given FL varies with temperature (cold = lower true altitude)
Vertical separation maintained by common pressure datum, not true altitude
True altitude may be significantly below FL in cold air masses
Terrain clearance still matters -- minimum FLs take worst-case temperature into account
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM)
concept: Reduced Vertical Separation Minima (RVSM) is an ICAO scheme that halves the vertical separation between aircraft between FL290 and FL410 -- from 2,000 ft to 1,000 ft. To squeeze that many flight levels into the airspace between FL290 and FL410, the altimetry systems on board must be far more accurate than standard. RVSM-approved aircraft have certified altimetry with tighter tolerances. Without approval, you can't operate in RVSM airspace.
exam_tip: RVSM = 1,000 ft vertical separation between FL290 and FL410 (instead of the standard 2,000 ft). Requires special aircraft and operator approval.
key_facts: RVSM reduces vertical separation from 2,000 ft to 1,000 ft
Applies between FL290 and FL410 inclusive
Doubles the number of usable cruising levels in that band
Requires certified altimetry accuracy and operational approval
Implemented globally under ICAO Doc 9574
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.8

syllabus_desc: Describe the extent of RVSM airspace
concept: In New Zealand, RVSM airspace covers both the New Zealand FIR and the Auckland Oceanic FIR. The vertical extent is FL290 to FL410 inclusive. So if you're cruising at FL350 on a trans-Tasman or trans-Pacific route, you're in RVSM airspace. Your aircraft must be RVSM approved, your crew trained, and the approval indicated in item 10 of the ICAO flight plan with the designator 'W'.
exam_tip: NZ RVSM airspace: FL290 to FL410, in both the New Zealand FIR and Auckland Oceanic FIR. Must file 'W' in item 10 of ICAO flight plan.
key_facts: RVSM applies: New Zealand FIR and Auckland Oceanic FIR
Vertical limits: FL290 (lower) to FL410 (upper)
ICAO flight plan: insert 'W' in item 10 if RVSM approved
Non-RVSM aircraft must include STS/NONRVSM in item 18
Non-RVSM aircraft plan to operate at or below FL280 as alternate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.8

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for operating in RVSM airspace
concept: To operate in RVSM airspace you need operational and airworthiness approval from your State of Registry or State of the Operator. For NZ-registered aircraft, AC 91-4 outlines the approval process. The aircraft altimetry must meet tighter accuracy standards, autopilot altitude-hold performance must meet minimum criteria, and the crew must be trained. Once approved, file 'W' in item 10 of your ICAO flight plan. You must notify ATC immediately if RVSM equipment becomes unserviceable while in -- or within 30 minutes of entering -- RVSM airspace. If TCAS is fitted, update to TCAS Change 7 or later for optimum RVSM performance.
exam_tip: RVSM operating requirements: approved altimetry, altitude-hold capability, trained crew, 'W' in item 10, notify ATC immediately if equipment fails. AC 91-4 is the NZ approval AC.
key_facts: Requires operational and airworthiness approval (State of Registry or Operator)
NZ: refer to AC 91-4 for approval process
Aircraft altimetry must meet RVSM accuracy tolerances
Autopilot altitude-hold must meet minimum performance criteria
Insert 'W' in item 10 of ICAO flight plan
Notify ATC if RVSM equipment unserviceable within or up to 30 min before RVSM airspace
TCAS should be updated to Change 7 or later if installed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.8, NZ AC 91-4

syllabus_desc: List the aeronautical charts used in New Zealand for operations under IFR
concept: For IFR operations in New Zealand, the main aeronautical charts you'll use are published in AIPNZ Vol 2 (Aerodrome/FIR) and Vol 3 (Instrument Procedures). The en route charts cover airways, reporting points, airspace, minimum altitudes, and VOR/DME facilities. The approach and departure plates cover SIDs, STARs, instrument approach procedures, missed approach procedures, and visual arrivals. New Zealand charts conform to ICAO Doc 8697 (Aeronautical Chart Manual). Digital equivalents are available via Jeppesen and other providers, but the official CAANZ source is the AIPNZ.
exam_tip: NZ IFR charts: primarily AIPNZ Vol 2 and Vol 3. En route charts, SIDs, STARs, approach plates all published there. ICAO Doc 8697 sets the standard.
key_facts: AIPNZ Vol 2: en route charts, airspace, FIR information
AIPNZ Vol 3: instrument procedures (SIDs, STARs, approaches)
En route charts show airways, MEA, MRA, MSA, airspace classes and reporting points
Charts conform to ICAO Doc 8697 standards
WGS 84 datum used on all NZ aeronautical charts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Identify the information published in the legends of aeronautical charts and in the CHART Symbols section of the AIPNZ Vol 2 & 3
concept: The legend of any aeronautical chart and the CHART Symbols section of AIPNZ Vol 2 & 3 publish the standard symbols, abbreviations, and colour codes used on NZ charts. This includes symbols for compulsory and non-compulsory reporting points, VOR/DME station symbols, airway identifiers, minimum altitudes, airways widths, controlled and uncontrolled airspace boundaries, and chart datum. Knowing how to read the legend is the starting point for chart interpretation -- if you can't decode the symbols, the rest of the chart is noise.
exam_tip: CHART Symbols section of AIPNZ Vol 2 & 3 = definitive legend for all NZ IFR charts. Know your symbols: compulsory vs non-compulsory reporting points, airway identifiers, altitude boxes, airspace boundaries.
key_facts: Legends published in AIPNZ Vol 2 & 3 CHART Symbols section
Covers symbols for reporting points (compulsory triangle, non-compulsory open triangle)
VOR/DME station symbols, airway identifiers (e.g. Y, A, B airways)
Minimum altitude boxes (MEA, MRA, MSA, MFA)
Airspace class designators and boundary depictions
Controlled and uncontrolled airspace colour coding
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of abbreviations and codes used in Operational Data for aerodromes in the AIPNZ
concept: Operational Data sections for aerodromes in the AIPNZ use a set of standard abbreviations and codes -- ICAO location indicator, elevation, runway details (TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA), navigation aid frequencies, lighting categories (HIRL, MIRL, LIRL, REIL), and aerodrome operating times. You need to be able to decode these quickly in a planning context. For example, 'EL LVPS OBST LGTS -- Elevated power supply, obstacle lights' -- that sort of thing. The AIPNZ GEN 2.2 section lists standard abbreviations used throughout.
exam_tip: AIPNZ Operational Data abbreviations: know runway declared distances (TORA/TODA/ASDA/LDA), lighting codes, and how to find frequencies and services. GEN 2.2 = abbreviations list.
key_facts: TORA: take-off run available; TODA: take-off distance available; ASDA: accelerate-stop distance available; LDA: landing distance available
HIRL/MIRL/LIRL: high/medium/low intensity runway lights
ICAO 4-letter location indicator (e.g. NZCH = Christchurch)
Standard abbreviations listed in GEN 2.2
Aerodrome elevation in feet AMSL
Nav aid frequencies listed in aerodrome entry or in ENR charts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ GEN 2.2

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: ADEP
concept: ADEP is the ICAO abbreviation for 'aerodrome of departure'. On IFR flight plans and ATC clearances, ADEP identifies the origin aerodrome using its 4-letter ICAO location indicator. For example, ADEP NZCH means departing Christchurch International.
exam_tip: ADEP = Aerodrome of Departure. 4-letter ICAO code used on flight plans and clearances.
key_facts: ADEP = Aerodrome of Departure
Identified by 4-letter ICAO location indicator
Used in flight plan item 13 (departure aerodrome)
Example: ADEP NZCH = departing Christchurch
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, ICAO Doc 4444

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: ADES
concept: ADES is the ICAO abbreviation for 'aerodrome of destination'. It appears in flight plan item 16 and in ATC clearances. The ADES is the planned final destination, not an intermediate stop. If your planned destination is Queenstown, your ADES is NZQN.
exam_tip: ADES = Aerodrome of Destination. 4-letter ICAO code. Appears in flight plan item 16.
key_facts: ADES = Aerodrome of Destination
Identified by 4-letter ICAO location indicator
Flight plan item 16 = destination aerodrome and alternate(s)
Example: ADES NZQN = Queenstown International as destination
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, ICAO Doc 4444

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: waypoint
concept: A waypoint is a defined geographical position used for defining a route or flight profile. Waypoints can be defined by latitude/longitude, by a VOR radial/distance, or by a named fix on a chart. On IFR routes, waypoints replace or supplement traditional nav aids as reporting points and turning points. In the GNSS era, waypoints are the backbone of PBN routes, SIDs, and STARs.
exam_tip: Waypoint = defined geographical position. Can be lat/long, VOR radial/distance, or named fix. Used as reporting points and turning points on IFR routes.
key_facts: A defined geographical position used to describe a route
Defined by lat/long, or as a VOR radial/distance fix
Named waypoints appear on en route charts and procedure plates
Used as compulsory or non-compulsory reporting points
Core element of RNAV/PBN routes and procedures
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, ICAO Doc 4444, ICAO Doc 9613

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: SID
concept: A Standard Instrument Departure (SID) is a published instrument flight procedure that connects the aerodrome to the en route structure. It gives IFR departures a defined path out of the terminal area that ensures obstacle clearance and separates them from arrivals. ATC issues a SID clearance by name and any altitude amendments. If the SID includes a climb gradient, the gradient is expressed as a percentage or as a rate of climb at a specified speed. On departing Christchurch to the north, for example, you'd follow the published SID until reaching the en route joining fix.
exam_tip: SID = Standard Instrument Departure. Published procedure from aerodrome to en route structure. Provides obstacle clearance and traffic separation. Climb gradients may be specified.
key_facts: SID = Standard Instrument Departure
Connects aerodrome to en route structure
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3 as plates with plan view and textual instructions
Provides obstacle clearance during departure phase
May include minimum climb gradient (expressed as % or ft/nm)
ATC clears by SID name (e.g. 'Cleared NZCH via CASHMERE ONE departure')
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: STAR
concept: A Standard Arrival Route (STAR) is the mirror of the SID -- a published procedure that connects the en route structure to the terminal area and eventually to the approach. It sequences arriving traffic into the approach environment, handles speed and altitude constraints, and provides a predictable path that ATC can sequence. ATC may issue a STAR clearance by name, and pilots are expected to comply with published altitudes and speeds unless amended.
exam_tip: STAR = Standard Arrival Route. Connects en route structure to approach. Published altitude and speed constraints must be complied with unless ATC amends.
key_facts: STAR = Standard Terminal Arrival Route
Published procedure from en route to terminal area
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3
Altitude and speed constraints are mandatory unless ATC amends
Facilitates traffic sequencing in terminal area
ATC clears by STAR name (e.g. 'Cleared WOODBOURNE STAR')
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: minimum en route altitude (MEA)
concept: The Minimum En Route Altitude (MEA) in the New Zealand system is the lowest altitude at which adequate NDB signal can be received on an NDB route. This is the AIP NZ GEN 2.2 definition and is narrower than the generic ICAO MEA (which also bundles obstacle clearance into the definition). In NZ, obstacle clearance on an airway segment is handled separately through the sector MSA / MFA / terrain clearance rules under CAR 91.423, while the MEA itself is purely a signal-reception floor for NDB-based routes. KDR feedback and ASPEQ exam questions use the AIP NZ wording.
exam_tip: AIP NZ GEN 2.2: MEA = lowest altitude at which adequate NDB signal can be received on an NDB route. It is NOT the ICAO definition bundling signal + obstacle clearance -- in NZ those are separate concepts.
key_facts: MEA: Minimum En Route Altitude (AIP NZ GEN 2.2)
Lowest altitude for adequate NDB signal reception on an NDB route
Narrower than ICAO MEA -- does NOT include obstacle clearance in the NZ definition
Obstacle clearance handled separately via MSA/MFA and CAR 91.423
ASPEQ KDR feedback uses the AIP NZ wording -- not the ICAO/FAA one
source: AIP NZ GEN 2.2; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Corrected per AIP NZ authoritative source after courseware audit 2026-04-09.

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: minimum reception altitude (MRA)
concept: The Minimum Reception Altitude (MRA) in the New Zealand system is the lowest altitude at which adequate VOR signal can be received on a VOR route. This is the AIP NZ GEN 2.2 definition -- purely a signal-reception floor, not an obstacle clearance altitude. It is narrower than the ICAO/FAA definition (which frames MRA as the lowest altitude to receive an off-route cross-radial to identify a fix). In NZ, MRA refers to on-route VOR signal reception along a VOR airway.
exam_tip: AIP NZ GEN 2.2: MRA = lowest altitude at which adequate VOR signal can be received on a VOR route. Purely a reception constraint. Do NOT use the ICAO cross-radial-fix definition in NZ exams.
key_facts: MRA: Minimum Reception Altitude (AIP NZ GEN 2.2)
Lowest altitude for adequate VOR signal reception on a VOR route
NZ definition is narrower than the ICAO off-route fix definition
Purely a radio reception constraint -- not obstacle clearance
ASPEQ KDR feedback uses the AIP NZ wording
source: AIP NZ GEN 2.2; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Corrected per AIP NZ authoritative source after courseware audit 2026-04-09.

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: minimum safe altitude (MSA)
concept: In the New Zealand system, the term Minimum Safe Altitude (MSA) refers to the lowest altitude that provides required obstacle clearance under CAR 91.423. This is the AIP NZ GEN 2.2 definition. It is NOT the ICAO 'minimum SECTOR altitude' -- that concept exists in NZ as well but is labelled MSA 25 NM (the 25 NM sector-based altitude on an approach-plate MSA diagram, guaranteeing 1000 ft obstacle clearance within sectors centred on a navaid or aerodrome reference point). Getting these two confused is a common KDR issue.
exam_tip: NZ AIP GEN 2.2: MSA = lowest altitude providing obstacle clearance under CAR 91.423. This is NOT the ICAO minimum sector altitude -- in NZ that is called MSA 25 NM. Watch the distinction on exam stems.
key_facts: NZ MSA: lowest altitude providing CAR 91.423 obstacle clearance (AIP NZ GEN 2.2)
MSA 25 NM (separate term) = 25 NM sector altitude with 1000 ft obstacle clearance
MSA 25 NM is the ICAO 'minimum sector altitude' concept
Do NOT answer with the 25 NM sector definition for a plain 'MSA' question in a NZ exam
Both concepts sit in AIP NZ GEN 2.2 and are differentiated explicitly
source: AIP NZ GEN 2.2; CAR 91.423; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Corrected per AIP NZ authoritative source after courseware audit 2026-04-09.

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: route operating limitations (ROL)
concept: Route Operating Limitations (ROL) are restrictions placed on specific airway segments or routes -- they might restrict certain aircraft types, limit hours of operation, specify equipment requirements, or require ATC authorisation. ROLs appear in the AIPNZ and on en route charts as notes or symbols. Always check for ROLs when flight planning an IFR route -- an unanticipated restriction mid-route is never fun.
exam_tip: ROL = Route Operating Limitation. Restrictions on a specific airway segment (aircraft type, hours, equipment, ATC authorisation). Check AIPNZ notes on en route charts.
key_facts: ROL: Route Operating Limitation
Restricts use of a specific airway or route segment
May limit aircraft type, operating hours, equipage, or require prior ATC approval
Published in AIPNZ and noted on en route charts
Must be checked during IFR flight planning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: minimum flight altitude (MFA)
concept: The Minimum Flight Altitude (MFA) is defined in AIP NZ GEN 2.2 as the lowest level at or above the route sector minimum safe altitude, MRA, MEA, or upper limit of a Volcanic Hazard Zone or Danger/Restricted Area (whichever is highest), in accordance with the direction of flight as prescribed in CAR 91.425. The MFA therefore rolls up the highest relevant minimum for the segment being flown. It is NOT limited to standard-route-clearance segments -- the MFA concept applies on any IFR segment.
exam_tip: AIP NZ GEN 2.2 MFA = highest of (route sector MSA / MRA / MEA / upper limit of VHZ or D/R area), applied with direction of flight per CAR 91.425. MFA is general, not limited to standard routes.
key_facts: MFA: Minimum Flight Altitude (AIP NZ GEN 2.2)
Highest of: route sector MSA, MRA, MEA, upper limit of VHZ, upper limit of D/R area
Applied with direction of flight per CAR 91.425 (odd/even semi-circular)
NOT limited to standard routes -- applies to any IFR flight segment
Pilot-calculated floor that rolls up all relevant segment minima
source: AIP NZ GEN 2.2; CAR 91.425; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Corrected per AIP NZ authoritative source after courseware audit 2026-04-09.

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: compulsory reporting point
concept: A compulsory reporting point (CRP) is a position at which a pilot is required to make an ATC position report when operating on that route. On en route charts it's shown as a solid triangle (filled). At a CRP you report: callsign, position, time over, altitude, next reporting point and estimated time. Compulsory reports are required without ATC prompting -- they're part of the IFR radio protocol and keep ATC aware of your position in airspace where radar coverage may be limited.
exam_tip: Compulsory reporting point = solid triangle symbol on chart. Must report without ATC prompting: callsign, position, time, altitude, next point and ETA.
key_facts: Compulsory Reporting Point: pilot must report without ATC request
Symbol: solid filled triangle on en route chart
Required report content: callsign, position, time, altitude, next reporting point, ETA next point
Mandatory in all airspace where position reports are required
Critical for separation in non-radar airspace
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: non-compulsory reporting point
concept: A non-compulsory reporting point (NCRP) is a position where a position report is only made on request from ATC, or at the pilot's discretion. On en route charts it's shown as an open (unfilled) triangle. While not mandatory, these points are still useful references and ATC may request a report at them, particularly if they need additional traffic separation information.
exam_tip: Non-compulsory reporting point = open (unfilled) triangle. Report only on ATC request or pilot's discretion.
key_facts: Non-Compulsory Reporting Point: report on ATC request or pilot discretion
Symbol: open (unfilled) triangle on en route chart
ATC may request a report here for separation purposes
Pilot may self-initiate report if useful for safety
Less critical in radar-covered airspace but still charted
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: VOR change-over point
concept: The VOR change-over point (COP) is the point along an airway segment where a pilot is expected to transfer primary navigation reference from the VOR behind them to the VOR ahead. It's typically at the midpoint but may be shifted if signal quality, terrain, or coverage requires it. On en route charts it's marked with a 'X' symbol with the COP distances to each VOR. Flying past the COP without switching can lead to greater position error as the behind-VOR signal geometry deteriorates.
exam_tip: VOR change-over point = where you switch from the behind VOR to the ahead VOR. Usually midpoint but may be offset. Marked 'X' with distances on chart.
key_facts: VOR Change-over Point: transfer primary nav reference from rear to forward VOR
Marked on en route chart with 'X' symbol and distances to each VOR
Usually at midpoint of VOR-to-VOR segment but may be offset
Beyond COP: rear VOR signal geometry less accurate, forward VOR more reliable
Pilot is expected to switch without ATC instruction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms presented on en route charts: Distance steps
concept: Distance steps (also called 'enroute descent (Distance) steps' in AIP NZ ENR 1.05 and ENR 1.1 §8.8.5-8.8.9) are stepped minimum altitudes tied to DME distance from a reference navaid. They are used on both departures (REVERSE DME STEP procedures) and arrivals to give a progressive minimum-altitude profile -- at DME X you must be at or above altitude Y, at DME X+5 you must be at or above Y+Z, and so on. Distance steps are published on en route charts, SIDs, and STARs. They are NOT cumulative track miles for waypoint identification -- that is a different chart feature.
exam_tip: Distance steps = stepped minimum altitudes based on DME from a reference navaid. AIP NZ ENR 1.05 and ENR 1.1 §8.8.5-8.8.9. Used for REVERSE DME STEP departures and progressive descent on arrival. NOT a cumulative track-miles tool.
key_facts: Stepped minimum altitudes linked to DME distance from a reference navaid
Published in AIP NZ ENR 1.05 and ENR 1.1 §8.8.5-8.8.9
Used on departures (REVERSE DME STEP) and arrivals for progressive minima
Typical format: at X DME, minimum altitude Y; at X+5 DME, minimum altitude Y+Z
Gisborne example: 271 radial H328, 3000 ft to CREEK (15 nm), 5600 ft by 20 DME, 6400 ft by 30 DME
NOT cumulative track miles for waypoint identification
GNSS may not be used for distance step procedures unless pilot has a distance readout from the reference point (AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.8)
source: AIP NZ ENR 1.05; AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.5-8.8.9; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Corrected per AIP NZ authoritative source after courseware audit 2026-04-09.

syllabus_desc: With regard to Standard Routes, describe in detail: the function of the routes
concept: Standard routes in New Zealand are predefined IFR routes between aerodromes that have been designed by Airways New Zealand and published in the AIPNZ. They serve as the backbone of IFR traffic flow in NZ -- defining which airways, waypoints, altitudes, and procedures connect major aerodromes. The function is to simplify clearance delivery, reduce RT workload, and ensure that IFR traffic follows a predictable, obstacle-cleared route. Instead of spelling out the entire routing, ATC issues a standard route clearance by its short designator.
exam_tip: Standard routes: predefined IFR routes between aerodromes, published in AIPNZ, cleared by short designator. Reduce RT workload and simplify IFR clearances.
key_facts: Predefined IFR routes between NZ aerodromes, designed by Airways NZ
Published in AIPNZ ENR 1.1 and on en route charts
Function: simplify clearance delivery, reduce RT, ensure obstacle clearance
ATC issues clearance using the standard route designator
Pilot is responsible for knowing the full routing associated with the designator
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: With regard to Standard Routes, describe in detail: the associated standard route clearance system
concept: The standard route clearance system allows ATC to issue an entire IFR routing with a short designator rather than reading out every waypoint and altitude. Clearance format: '[callsign] cleared [ADEP] to [ADES] via [designator] departure, [standard route designator], [arrival], [FL/altitude], squawk [code]'. The pilot must know exactly what that designator means -- the full routing is published in the AIPNZ. If the clearance modifies any element of the standard route, ATC will state the amendment explicitly.
exam_tip: Standard route clearance: ATC issues by short designator. Pilot must know the full routing. Any amendments are stated explicitly. Full routing in AIPNZ ENR 1.1.
key_facts: ATC issues clearance by standard route designator -- short and efficient
Pilot responsibility: know the full routing published for that designator
Clearance includes: departure, standard route designator, arrival, level, squawk
Amendments to the standard route must be stated explicitly by ATC
Multiple route options may be contained in one standard route clearance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: With regard to Standard Routes, describe in detail: the manner in which standard routes are highlighted on en route charts
concept: Standard routes are highlighted on NZ en route charts with a thicker line or highlighted/bolded airway depiction, and the standard route designator is printed alongside. The highlighting makes it easy to visually trace the standard route on the chart and cross-check your clearance. Standard route segments shown on the chart also display MEA, airway identifier, and any ROLs that apply.
exam_tip: Standard routes on en route charts: bold/highlighted airway lines with the route designator printed alongside. Visual aid to trace your clearance on the chart.
key_facts: Shown on en route charts with bolded or highlighted airway depiction
Standard route designator printed alongside the highlighted route
Allows visual tracing of the cleared routing
MEA and airway identifiers shown for each segment
ROLs and any restrictions also noted on the chart
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With regard to Standard Routes, describe in detail: the designator allocated to individual standard routes
concept: Standard routes in New Zealand use a coded designator system. The designator identifies both the origin aerodrome and the destination aerodrome, and may include a number to distinguish between multiple routes between the same pair of aerodromes. For example, 'CH4' might designate a specific standard route from Christchurch, number 4. The designator is used in the ATC clearance and in the AIPNZ text.
exam_tip: Standard route designators are coded to reflect the aerodrome pair and route number. Used in ATC clearance and AIPNZ ENR 1.1 tables.
key_facts: Designator encodes origin/destination and route number
Used verbatim in ATC clearance
Published in AIPNZ ENR 1.1 standard route tables
Multiple designators may exist between the same city pair
Pilot must be able to decode the designator and trace the full routing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: With regard to Standard Routes, describe in detail: the documents where standard routes are published
concept: Standard routes are published in AIPNZ ENR 1.1 (General Rules and Procedures), in the standard route clearance tables. These tables list the designator, the full routing, altitudes, and any restrictions. The routes are also depicted on NZ en route charts (AIPNZ Vol 2). Jeppesen and other chart providers also reflect standard routes, but the primary and authoritative source is the AIPNZ.
exam_tip: Standard routes published in: AIPNZ ENR 1.1 (text tables) and AIPNZ Vol 2 en route charts (graphical). AIPNZ is the authoritative source.
key_facts: Primary publication: AIPNZ ENR 1.1 (standard route tables)
Graphical depiction: AIPNZ Vol 2 en route charts
Third-party providers (Jeppesen etc.) also publish but AIPNZ is authoritative
Tables include: designator, full routing, MEAs, ROLs, and notes
Amended by AIRACs and NOTAMs
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: With regard to uncharted routes, state: the document, and section, where the routes are published
concept: Uncharted routes (also called random routes) in New Zealand are published in AIPNZ ENR 1.1, in the section dealing with IFR flight on uncharted routes. These are IFR flights that don't follow a published airway or standard route -- the pilot plans a direct track between waypoints. The ENR 1.1 section sets out the conditions, reporting requirements, and altitude constraints for uncharted route operations.
exam_tip: Uncharted routes (random routes) published in AIPNZ ENR 1.1. Section on IFR flight on uncharted routes covers conditions, reporting, and altitudes.
key_facts: Uncharted routes: IFR flights not on published airways or standard routes
Published in AIP NZ ENR 1.1 (general rules for uncharted route IFR)
Sometimes called random routes
Position reporting still required at specified intervals or waypoints
Altitude must provide obstacle clearance -- no published MEA to rely on
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: With regard to uncharted routes, state: the designator allocated to the routes
concept: Company-generated uncharted routes in New Zealand use a designator that typically includes a 'Z' prefix (e.g. Z1, Z2) or a similar code that distinguishes them from published standard route designators. The specific designator format is agreed with Airways New Zealand. Individual operators may have their own designator prefixes approved for use in ATC clearances. Pilots must be briefed on what the uncharted route designator means for their operation.
exam_tip: Uncharted routes: typically Z-prefixed designators (e.g. Z1) or operator-specific codes agreed with Airways NZ. Not found on published charts -- operators must brief pilots.
key_facts: Uncharted route designators: typically Z-prefix or operator-specific coding
Agreed with Airways New Zealand
Not published on standard en route charts
Operator must ensure pilots are briefed on routing
Used in ATC clearance in lieu of full waypoint description
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: With regard to uncharted routes, state: the meaning of chart symbols (e.g. asterisks)
concept: On some NZ en route charts, an asterisk (*) is used to indicate that a waypoint or route point is not coincident with a named nav aid or published compulsory reporting point -- it's a company-specific or operator-defined point. When you see an asterisk on a chart next to a position, it typically means the waypoint definition is published elsewhere (not on the chart itself) or that special conditions apply. In the context of uncharted routes, asterisks may flag that a waypoint on a filed uncharted route needs to be cross-referenced from the operator's route documents.
exam_tip: Asterisk on en route chart: the point is not a standard nav aid or published reporting point -- cross-reference operator route docs or AIPNZ notes. Look for the footnote.
key_facts: Asterisk (*): waypoint not coincident with standard nav aid or published point
Requires cross-reference to route documentation or AIPNZ notes
Used to flag operator-defined or non-standard waypoints
Always check the chart footnote or legend for asterisk meaning
Common on company-specific routes filed as uncharted routes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Determine on appropriate en route charts: the magnetic tracks and distances of route segments
concept: Magnetic tracks and distances of route segments are printed directly on NZ en route charts alongside each airway or direct route segment. The magnetic track is the direction you fly (in degrees magnetic) and the distance is in nautical miles. These are the two primary pieces of information you need to build a navigation plan. On airways, both directions are usually shown (e.g. 045/225 for a northeast/southwest airway). Always use the track appropriate to your direction of flight.
exam_tip: Magnetic tracks and segment distances are published on en route charts alongside each airway segment. Use the track for your direction of travel.
key_facts: Magnetic track (degrees M) printed alongside each airway segment on the chart
Distance in nautical miles shown for each segment
Both reciprocal directions usually shown (e.g. 045/225)
Use the track appropriate to your direction of flight
Distances are great circle distances, not rhumb line (for long segments)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Determine on appropriate en route charts: the type or class of airspace in which an IFR flight is operating
concept: The airspace class through which your IFR route passes is shown on en route charts using the ICAO classification system (A, B, C, D, E, G). Class A is the most restrictive (all IFR, radar mandatory, ATC separation provided). New Zealand has Class A, C, D, E, and G airspace -- no Class B or F. The airspace class symbol appears in the airspace boundary or alongside the controlling authority identifier. Knowing your airspace class tells you what separation service you can expect and what communication and equipment requirements apply.
exam_tip: Airspace class shown on en route charts. NZ has A, C, D, E, G. Class A = all IFR, full ATC separation. G = uncontrolled. Equipment and comms requirements differ by class.
key_facts: ICAO airspace classes shown on en route charts: A, C, D, E, G in NZ
Class A: IFR only, ATC separation to all, continuous comms required
Class C: IFR and VFR, ATC separation to IFR, comms required
Class D: IFR and VFR, ATC separation to IFR, comms required
Class E: IFR and VFR, ATC separation to IFR only
Class G: uncontrolled, ATC separation not provided
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.4

syllabus_desc: Determine on appropriate en route charts: airspace boundaries
concept: Airspace boundaries are depicted on en route charts using lateral boundary lines (often with class designators and controlling authority labels). In New Zealand, controlled airspace above transition altitude is primarily Class A. Control zones (CTZs) around aerodromes are shown with their lateral limits and vertical extents. Boundaries can be defined by latitude/longitude, by distance from a navaid, or by radial/arc. Always check that your planned route stays within or outside the correct airspace class for your operations.
exam_tip: Airspace boundaries: depicted on chart with boundary lines, class designators, and controlling authority labels. Lateral limits may be defined by coordinates, VOR radials, or arcs.
key_facts: Shown on chart with boundary lines and airspace class labels
Controlled airspace above transition: primarily Class A in NZ
CTZ (Control Zone) lateral limits typically distance from ARP
Boundaries defined by coordinates, VOR radials, DME arcs, or geographic features
Critical for flight planning: must remain within filed airspace class
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 2.1

syllabus_desc: Determine on appropriate en route charts: airspace vertical limits
concept: Airspace vertical limits on NZ en route charts are expressed in feet AMSL (for altitudes below transition) or as flight levels (above transition). They're shown as the upper and lower limits of a particular airspace block, usually in the format 'base/top' -- e.g. SFC/FL245 or 3,500 ft/FL150. For controlled airspace, the vertical limits define exactly where ATC has jurisdiction and where the associated separation service applies.
exam_tip: Vertical limits: shown as base/top on the chart. Below transition in ft AMSL, above in FLs. Example: SFC/FL245 = surface to FL245.
key_facts: Expressed as base/top, e.g. SFC/FL150 or 3,500 ft/FL245
Below transition: altitudes in feet AMSL
Above transition: expressed as flight levels
SFC = surface (ground level)
UNL = unlimited upper limit (no ceiling)
Defines where ATC separation service applies
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 2.1

syllabus_desc: Determine on appropriate en route charts: airspace controlling authority
concept: The controlling authority (or 'agency') for each airspace block is shown on the en route chart, usually alongside the airspace class designator or in a label box. In New Zealand, Airways NZ operates the en route and terminal ATC services. The controlling authority label identifies which ATC frequency and unit manages that airspace -- critical for knowing which agency to call for clearances and separation services.
exam_tip: Controlling authority label on chart = ATC agency responsible for that airspace. In NZ, usually Airways NZ. Frequency published in AIPNZ or on the chart.
key_facts: Shown on chart alongside airspace class or in label box
In NZ: primarily Airways New Zealand
Identifies which ATC unit to contact for clearances
ATC frequency for that unit published in AIPNZ or on the chart
Terminal areas may have different controlling authority to en route
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With regard to the World Geodetic System 1984 (WGS 84) datum, state where this datum is published
concept: The World Geodetic System 1984 (WGS 84) is the datum used for all NZ aeronautical charts and GNSS navigation. In the NZ AIP, the use of WGS 84 as the chart datum is published in AIP NZ GEN 2.4 (Chart Datums) and in GEN 3.2 (Aeronautical Charts). All latitude/longitude coordinates on NZ IFR charts are referenced to WGS 84 and are directly compatible with GPS/GNSS-derived positions, which output in WGS 84 natively.
exam_tip: WGS 84 is the NZ chart datum. Published reference: AIP NZ GEN 2.4 (Chart Datums) and GEN 3.2 (Aeronautical Charts). Do NOT answer GEN 2.2 -- that is the abbreviations section.
key_facts: WGS 84 = World Geodetic System 1984
Global earth-centred ellipsoidal reference system
Published as NZ chart datum in AIP NZ GEN 2.4 (Chart Datums) and GEN 3.2 (Aeronautical Charts)
All NZ IFR chart coordinates referenced to WGS 84
GNSS outputs in WGS 84 natively -- directly compatible with NZ charts
source: AIP NZ GEN 2.4; AIP NZ GEN 3.2; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Corrected per AIP NZ authoritative source after courseware audit 2026-04-09.

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in VOR/DME MRA Sector (VORSEC) charts published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: VOR/DME MRA Sector (VORSEC) charts show the minimum altitudes required to receive specific VOR/DME signals in each sector around a navaid. They're divided into sectors radiating from the VOR/DME, each labelled with the altitude at which reliable reception is achievable. If you're flying en route using a VOR and you're below the VORSEC minimum for your sector, you won't get a reliable signal. VORSEC charts help you plan climbs before relying on a particular navaid for navigation.
exam_tip: VORSEC chart: sector altitudes for reliable VOR/DME reception around a navaid. Each sector shows the minimum altitude for signal reception. Below minimum = unreliable navaid.
key_facts: VORSEC = VOR/DME MRA Sector chart
Shows minimum altitude for reliable VOR/DME reception in each sector
Sectors radiate from the VOR/DME station
Altitude constraint is based on line-of-sight radio horizon
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3
Critical for planning when climbing or descending while using a VOR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in 25 DME Minimum Sector Altitude diagrams published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: The 25 DME Minimum Sector Altitude diagram is a standard diagram centred on a navaid showing the minimum safe altitude in each sector within a 25 NM radius. It guarantees 1,000 ft of obstacle clearance and ensures reliable VOR/DME reception in each sector. The diagram is divided into sectors (usually quadrants) with each sector altitude shown. It's a primary emergency and planning tool -- if you're within 25 DME and lose situational awareness, the MSA gives you a safe altitude to target.
exam_tip: 25 DME MSA diagram: 1,000 ft obstacle clearance + navaid reception within 25 NM, in each sector. Emergency reference altitude.
key_facts: 25 NM radius from navaid, divided into sectors (usually quadrants)
Each sector: 1,000 ft obstacle clearance guaranteed
Also ensures VOR/DME reception in that sector
Published on approach plates (AIPNZ Vol 3)
Emergency reference -- not a planned cruise altitude
Pilot must know which sector they're in (relative to navaid)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in Standard Instrument Departure (SID) diagrams published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: SID diagrams in the AIPNZ show the graphical route of the departure procedure including heading, turns, waypoints, crossing altitudes, and speed restrictions. The plan view (top-down) shows the lateral path. A textual description on the plate defines the procedure step by step, including initial climbing turn, fly headings, intercept radials or tracks, and join the en route structure. Minimum climb gradients (expressed as %, ft/nm or minimum climb rate at a given airspeed) are published if terrain requires a steeper than standard 200 ft/nm departure gradient.
exam_tip: SID diagram: plan view of lateral path + text description of procedure steps. Check for minimum climb gradient requirements -- may be steeper than standard.
key_facts: Plan view shows lateral path, waypoints, heading constraints
Textual description provides step-by-step procedure
Minimum climb gradient published if required (standard is 200 ft/nm = 3.3%)
Speed restrictions may be shown (e.g. 'maintain 210 kt or less to waypoint')
Crossing altitudes at specific waypoints shown
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in Standard Arrival Route (STAR) charts published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: STAR charts in the AIPNZ show the published arrival route from the en route structure to the initial approach fix (IAF). The plan view shows waypoints, tracks, and any speed/altitude constraints. A STAR may serve multiple runways with a common initial track until a certain point, or may be runway-specific. Crossing altitudes ('at or above', 'at or below', or 'at') are mandatory unless amended by ATC. Speed constraints on a STAR must also be complied with as published.
exam_tip: STAR chart: arrival route from en route to IAF. Altitude and speed constraints are mandatory (unless ATC amends). 'At', 'at or above', 'at or below' are all different -- read carefully.
key_facts: Shows route from en route structure to Initial Approach Fix (IAF)
Plan view: waypoints, tracks, altitude constraints, speed restrictions
Altitude constraints: 'at', 'at or above', 'at or below' -- all binding unless amended
Speed constraints: comply as published unless ATC amends
May be common to multiple runways or runway-specific
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in visual arrival charts published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: Visual arrival charts (also called visual approach charts or VAC) depict the preferred visual arrival routes into an aerodrome. They show the relationship between visual landmarks (coastlines, rivers, mountains, roads), visual reporting points (VRPs), the traffic circuit, and the runway. They're not instrument procedures -- you need VMC to fly them. But on a day IFR flight that's breaking out into VMC near the destination, a visual arrival chart helps the pilot set up visually without fumbling for landmarks.
exam_tip: Visual arrival charts: used when transitioning from IFR to visual. Show VRPs, landmarks, circuit, runway. Require VMC -- not an instrument procedure.
key_facts: Show visual reporting points (VRPs), landmarks, circuit, and runway layout
Require visual meteorological conditions (VMC) -- not an instrument procedure
Useful for transitioning from IFR to visual near the destination
Depict preferred arrival routes relative to identifiable terrain features
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3 for applicable aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in instrument approach charts published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: Instrument approach charts contain: plan view (lateral path), profile view (vertical path), minima table (MDA/DH, visibility), and missed approach procedure. They define the procedure from the IAF through the intermediate approach, final approach, and missed approach or landing. The minima table lists MDAs or DHs for various aircraft categories (A through D) and in some cases for different equipment levels (e.g. ILS, baro-VNAV, LNAV). The missed approach instructions are mandatory if minima are not met or not visible.
exam_tip: Instrument approach chart: plan view + profile view + minima table + missed approach. Minima vary by aircraft category (A-D) and approach type (ILS/LNAV/VNAV etc.).
key_facts: Plan view: lateral path from IAF to runway/missed approach point
Profile view: vertical path, step-down fixes, glideslope or step altitudes
Minima table: MDA (non-precision) or DH (precision), visibility by category
Aircraft categories A/B/C/D based on approach speed (1.3 x Vso)
Missed approach procedure published -- must execute if visual ref not obtained
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3 for each instrument-equipped runway
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in ground movement charts published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: Ground movement charts (sometimes called aerodrome charts or taxi charts) show the layout of runways, taxiways, aprons, parking bays, and hotspots at an aerodrome. They're used for taxi planning and to support compliance with ATC ground movement instructions. In NZ, ground movement charts are particularly useful at complex aerodromes like Christchurch (NZCH) or Auckland (NZAA) where multiple runway crossings and complex taxi routes exist.
exam_tip: Ground movement chart: aerodrome taxiway layout for taxi planning and ATC compliance. Published in AIPNZ Vol 3 for applicable aerodromes.
key_facts: Show runways, taxiways, aprons, holding points, and hotspots
Used for taxi planning and to cross-check ATC ground movement instructions
Include runway crossing points and hotspot warnings
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3
Important at complex aerodromes (NZCH, NZAA, NZWN)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in instrument T/O procedure chart - rate of climb table published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: The instrument take-off procedure chart includes a rate of climb table that shows the minimum rate of climb required at various groundspeeds to achieve the published climb gradient requirement. If you can't achieve the required rate of climb, you cannot conduct the ITO in IMC unless alternative obstacle clearance can be assured. The table translates the gradient (%) into a ROC at a given GS -- for example, 3.3% at 90 kt GS = approximately 300 ft/min. Higher groundspeed at same gradient = higher ROC required.
exam_tip: ITO rate of climb table: converts published climb gradient (%) into required ROC at given groundspeed. If aircraft can't achieve required ROC at departure GS, cannot fly ITO in IMC.
key_facts: Rate of climb table published on instrument T/O procedure chart
Translates gradient (%) to required ROC (ft/min) at given GS
3.3% (200 ft/nm) is the standard minimum gradient
Higher GS at same gradient requires higher ROC
If ROC unachievable: cannot fly ITO in IMC without alternative obstacle protection
Tables published in AIPNZ Vol 3 for aerodromes with ITO procedures
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Interpret information contained in IFR alternate aerodrome minima table published in the AIPNZ VOL 2 & 3
concept: The IFR alternate aerodrome minima table in the AIPNZ lists the weather minima (ceiling and visibility) required for an aerodrome to be nominated as an IFR alternate. If the forecast weather at your planned alternate doesn't meet the tabulated minima at ETA plus/minus 1 hour, you can't use it as your alternate. The table is aerodrome-specific and approach-type-specific (lower minima for ILS, higher for NDB). This is one of the first things to check in IFR fuel and alternate planning.
exam_tip: IFR alternate minima table: ceiling + visibility required to nominate an aerodrome as alternate. Check forecast against table for ETA +/- 1 hour. Lower minima for precision approaches.
key_facts: Lists ceiling and visibility required to nominate an aerodrome as IFR alternate
Aerodrome-specific and approach-type-specific
ILS alternate minima lower than NDB or VOR
Forecast weather at alternate must meet minima for ETA +/- 1 hour
Published in AIPNZ Vol 3
CAR Part 91 governs IFR alternate requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain compliance procedures associated with VOR/DME MRA Sector (VORSEC) charts
concept: To comply with a VORSEC chart, you must not navigate using that VOR below the published sector altitude for your sector. Before descending below the VORSEC altitude, ensure you have established the next nav source or that you're within the published reception altitude. VORSEC altitudes are not obstacle clearance altitudes -- they're reception altitudes. However, if you're below the VORSEC minimum, your VOR-based navigation is unreliable, which is an indirect safety risk.
exam_tip: VORSEC compliance: don't use that VOR below the published sector altitude for your sector. It's a reception requirement, not obstacle clearance.
key_facts: Must stay at or above VORSEC sector altitude for reliable VOR/DME reception
Check which sector you're in relative to the navaid
Not an obstacle clearance altitude -- don't confuse with MSA
Descend below VORSEC only when another nav source is established
Particularly relevant during descent on approach when transitioning to lower altitudes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Explain compliance procedures associated with 25 DME Minimum Sector Altitude diagrams
concept: The 25 DME MSA diagram is a safety net. Compliance means not descending below the published MSA for your sector unless you're established on a published instrument procedure that provides lower obstacle clearance. If you lose situational awareness, the MSA gives you a safe altitude to climb to. You need to know which sector you're in (which quadrant relative to the navaid, using your DME and bearing). The 25 DME MSA is on the approach plate header -- quickly accessible during an approach brief.
exam_tip: 25 DME MSA compliance: know which sector you're in, don't descend below that sector's altitude outside of a published procedure. Emergency safety net.
key_facts: Don't descend below MSA for your sector unless established on a published procedure
Identify your sector: which quadrant relative to the navaid using DME and bearing
MSA is published on the approach plate header for quick reference
If uncertain of position: climb to MSA immediately
Does not replace MEA for en route; applies within 25 NM of the navaid
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Explain compliance procedures associated with Standard Instrument Departure (SID) diagrams
concept: SID compliance requires flying the published procedure exactly as depicted unless ATC issues specific amendments. This means following the lateral path (headings, tracks, turning points), crossing published altitudes at the right places, and complying with speed restrictions. If a minimum climb gradient is published, you must achieve it -- if you can't, you must not depart in IMC (or must take the alternative departure procedure if published). After completing the SID, you join the en route structure at the prescribed fix or level.
exam_tip: SID compliance: fly the procedure exactly unless ATC amends. Minimum climb gradient is mandatory. If you can't achieve it: don't depart IMC.
key_facts: Fly lateral path exactly as published (unless ATC amends)
Comply with all crossing altitudes and speed restrictions
Minimum climb gradient is mandatory -- not optional
Cannot depart IMC if unable to achieve required climb gradient
Join en route structure at the published terminus of the SID
ATC may issue direct routings or climb amendments -- expect and be ready
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Explain compliance procedures associated with Standard Arrival Route (STAR) charts
concept: STAR compliance means flying the published lateral route to the IAF, meeting all altitude constraints, and complying with speed restrictions -- all unless ATC issues an amendment. ATC can issue 'descend now' or 'maintain higher altitude' or 'direct to [fix]' to manage traffic. You must not descend below a STAR altitude constraint without ATC authorisation. On a STAR, 'at or above' means you can be higher but not lower; 'at or below' means you can be lower but not higher; 'at' means exactly that altitude.
exam_tip: STAR compliance: meet all altitude and speed constraints unless ATC amends. 'At or above' = not lower. 'At or below' = not higher. 'At' = exactly.
key_facts: Fly published route to IAF, meet all altitude constraints
Speed restrictions mandatory unless ATC amends
'At or above': can be higher, cannot be lower
'At or below': can be lower, cannot be higher
'At': exactly that altitude required
ATC may issue amendments -- read back and comply
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: Explain compliance procedures associated with visual arrival charts
concept: Visual arrival chart compliance means you must have visual reference to proceed on that arrival. You cannot commence a visual arrival in IMC and 'hope' to break out -- visual arrivals require VFR conditions or at least enough VMC to navigate visually. Once cleared for a visual arrival, you're responsible for terrain and obstacle clearance using visual cues. ATC may clear you for a visual arrival from the STAR; you then proceed to the aerodrome using the VRPs shown on the chart.
exam_tip: Visual arrival chart compliance: must be in VMC. You take terrain/obstacle responsibility. Cleared from STAR via visual arrival -- track to aerodrome using VRPs.
key_facts: Must have VMC to conduct visual arrival
Pilot takes terrain and obstacle clearance responsibility once visual
ATC may clear from STAR onto visual arrival
Follow VRPs shown on visual arrival chart to aerodrome
Coordinate with ATC if weather deteriorates -- may need to revert to instrument approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Explain compliance procedures associated with instrument approach charts
concept: Instrument approach chart compliance means conducting the approach exactly as depicted: fly to the IAF, fly the procedure turn or arc if published (unless cleared straight-in), intercept the final approach track, cross step-down fixes at or above minimum altitudes, and either land or execute the missed approach if the required visual reference isn't established at MDA/DH. Never descend below MDA without the required visual reference for the runway environment. Missed approach must be initiated at the missed approach point (MAP).
exam_tip: Instrument approach compliance: fly the procedure exactly. Never below MDA without visual reference. Execute missed approach at MAP if not visual. Follow published missed approach procedure.
key_facts: Fly to IAF, then follow published lateral and vertical path
Cross all step-down fixes at or above published altitudes
Reach MDA or DH -- level off and assess visual reference
Visual reference required: runway environment, approach lights, threshold, PAPI etc.
If not visual at MDA/DH by MAP: initiate missed approach immediately
Follow published missed approach to specified fix/altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain compliance procedures associated with ground movement charts
concept: Ground movement chart compliance means following the chart and ATC taxi instructions precisely. Never cross a runway holding point (solid line on taxiway) without explicit ATC clearance. At night or in low visibility, taxi slowly and cross-check the chart constantly. Use the chart to anticipate turn-offs and taxiway identifiers. Hotspots (marked on the chart) are areas of increased risk of runway incursion or collision -- exercise extra vigilance at these points.
exam_tip: Ground movement chart compliance: hold short of runway holding points unless explicitly cleared. Extra vigilance at hotspots. Use the chart to anticipate taxi routing.
key_facts: Never cross runway holding point without explicit ATC clearance
Runway holding points: solid and dashed lines across taxiway
Hotspots marked on chart -- increased vigilance required
Use the chart to anticipate turns and identify taxiway designators
Read back all runway crossing clearances
Low visibility operations require additional caution
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: Interpret meteorological information for IFR takeoff minima
concept: IFR takeoff minima in New Zealand are published in AIPNZ Vol 3 for each aerodrome with a published instrument departure. Meteorological minima for takeoff (visibility and cloud ceiling) are specified per runway and departure direction. If no specific minima are published, standard Part 91 minima apply. The aim is to ensure that, on a rejected takeoff, the pilot can safely return and land, and that during the climb-out on the SID, obstacle clearance is maintained. Interpret met reports (METAR) and forecasts (TAF) against the tabulated minima for your departure time.
exam_tip: IFR takeoff minima: published in AIPNZ Vol 3 for each runway. Interpret METAR/TAF against tabulated values for departure time. No published minima = standard Part 91 apply.
key_facts: Published in AIPNZ Vol 3 per runway/departure direction
Specify visibility and ceiling required for takeoff
Purpose: ensure RTL capability and SID obstacle clearance
Interpret METAR (actual) and TAF (forecast) against the minima
No published specific minima: refer to CAR Part 91 standard requirements
Night operations may have different (higher) minima
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Interpret meteorological information for IFR approach minima
concept: IFR approach minima are published in the minima table of each instrument approach chart. They define the Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA) for non-precision approaches or the Decision Height (DH) for precision approaches, plus the required flight visibility. Minima vary by aircraft category (A through D, based on 1.3 x Vso) and by approach type (ILS has lower minima than VOR or NDB). Interpret the TAF and forecast to determine whether the expected weather at your ETA will be at or above the published minima for your approach type and aircraft category.
exam_tip: IFR approach minima: MDA (non-precision) or DH (precision) + visibility. Varies by aircraft category (A-D) and approach type. Check TAF for ETA +/- 1 hour.
key_facts: Minima table on approach plate: MDA/DH and required visibility
Aircraft categories A/B/C/D based on 1.3 x Vso approach speed
Precision (ILS/RNP AR LPV): Decision Height (DH) -- must execute go-around at DH if not visual
Non-precision (VOR/NDB/GNSS LNAV): MDA -- level off and assess
Check forecast (TAF) at destination for ETA +/- 1 hour
Lower minima = more demanding approach type and/or better aircraft equipment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the compliance procedures involved during precision and non-precision instrument approaches
concept: Precision approaches (ILS, GLS, RNP AR with vertical guidance) use a Decision Height (DH) -- at DH the pilot must have the required visual reference or immediately execute a missed approach. No levelling off at DH; it's the 'go/no-go' decision point. Non-precision approaches (VOR, NDB, GNSS LNAV) use a Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA) -- the pilot descends to MDA, levels off, and continues at MDA until either obtaining the required visual reference and landing, or reaching the Missed Approach Point (MAP) and executing the missed approach. The key difference: DH is a decision point on the glidepath; MDA is a floor you hold at.
exam_tip: Precision approach = Decision Height (DH): immediate go-around if not visual at DH. Non-precision = MDA: level off and hold until visual or MAP. Don't descend below MDA.
key_facts: Precision: ILS, GLS, LPV -- uses Decision Height (DH)
At DH: must have required visual reference or immediately go around
No leveloff at DH on precision approach -- it's a decision point
Non-precision: VOR, NDB, LNAV -- uses Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA)
At MDA: level off, continue to MAP, land if visual or go around at MAP
Never descend below MDA on a non-precision approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the compliance procedures associated with published missed approaches
concept: The missed approach procedure is published on the instrument approach chart and must be executed whenever: the required visual reference is not established by MDA/DH, or when a safe landing cannot be made after establishing visual reference. The missed approach initiates at the MAP (missed approach point) on non-precision approaches, or at DH on precision approaches. The procedure specifies an initial climbing action (climb straight ahead, or turn to a specified heading), then tracks to a missed approach fix (usually a navaid or waypoint) at a published altitude. Execute missed approach without hesitation -- a baulked approach is always the right choice when in doubt.
exam_tip: Missed approach: mandatory if not visual at MDA/MAP or DH, or if safe landing not achievable. Follow published procedure: initial climb action + track to missed approach fix at published altitude.
key_facts: Must execute if: not visual at MDA/DH, or safe landing not achievable
Non-precision: initiate at MAP; precision: initiate at DH
Published procedure: initial climb action (straight or turn) + track to fix
Missed approach fix with holding altitude specified
Execute without hesitation -- don't linger at MDA hoping to see the runway
Notify ATC as soon as practicable after initiating missed approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: For the preparation of a flight plan, determine: route details, including reporting points and turning points
concept: Route details for an IFR flight plan include the full list of waypoints, airways, standard routes, departure procedures, arrival procedures, and turning points from ADEP to ADES. Each segment must be fully defined so there's no ambiguity. Reporting points (both compulsory and non-compulsory) along the route are identified so that position reports can be made at the right locations. Turning points are defined by waypoint identifiers or lat/long. On NZ airways, standard route designators replace long lists of individual waypoints.
exam_tip: Route details = full path ADEP to ADES: airways/waypoints, SID, STAR, compulsory/non-compulsory reporting points, turning points. Must be unambiguous in the flight plan.
key_facts: Full routing: SID, en route waypoints/airways, STAR, approach type
Each waypoint defined by name, lat/long, or radial/distance
Compulsory and non-compulsory reporting points identified
Turning points defined precisely
Standard route designators can compress many waypoints into one code
All route segments must be achievable at or above MEA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: For the preparation of a flight plan, determine: climb performance data including minimum climb gradients associated with published departure procedures
