concept: Climb performance data for IFR planning includes the aircraft's climb gradient capability and the minimum climb gradient required by the published SID. SID climb gradients are expressed as a percentage (ft per 100 ft of horizontal distance) or as ft/nm. The standard minimum is 200 ft/nm (3.3%) -- if you can't achieve this in the conditions on the day (temperature, pressure, weight), you must not depart in IMC or must take an alternative procedure. Also check whether the SID specifies a minimum climb rate at a specific speed (e.g. 'must achieve 200 ft/nm at 140 kt').
exam_tip: SID minimum climb gradient: standard = 200 ft/nm (3.3%). If aircraft can't achieve it (weight, temperature, pressure), cannot depart IMC. Check aircraft POH for actual gradient capability.
key_facts: Standard minimum SID climb gradient: 200 ft/nm (3.3%)
Higher gradients published where terrain requires it
Aircraft capability varies with weight, temperature, altitude, engine condition
200 ft/nm = ~325 ft/min at 90 kt GS; ~530 ft/min at 150 kt GS
Cannot depart IMC if gradient cannot be achieved
Alternative procedures (e.g. lower minima, different runway) may be published
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: For the preparation of a flight plan, determine: descent performance data including rate of descent required to arrive at a position at a stipulated altitude, or to comply with published arrival procedures
concept: Descent performance data for IFR planning means working backwards from your destination. You need to know the required rate of descent to arrive at the step-down fixes or the FAF at the published altitude, and the rate of descent needed on the final approach segment. For a 3-degree glidepath, the rule of thumb is groundspeed x 5 = approximate rate of descent in ft/min (e.g. 120 kt GS = 600 ft/min). Also account for STAR crossing altitudes -- if the STAR requires you at a specific altitude at a certain fix, calculate top of descent accordingly.
exam_tip: Descent planning: 3-degree path rule: GS x 5 = ROD in ft/min. Plan TOD to meet STAR crossing altitudes. Account for temperature effects on final approach segment.
key_facts: 3-degree glidepath rule: GS (kt) x 5 = approximate ROD (ft/min)
Calculate TOD to meet first STAR crossing altitude constraint
Account for all STAR step-down altitudes working backwards from the IAF
Ensure ROD achievable within aircraft performance limits
Different descent rates for cruise descent vs STAR vs final approach
Temperature effects: cold air -- true altitude lower than indicated on final
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: For the preparation of a flight plan, determine: fuel consumption details during climb, cruise, descent, and during diversion (if different)
concept: Fuel consumption is not constant through a flight -- it varies with altitude, power setting, weight, and temperature. In planning, split the fuel calculation into climb, cruise, and descent phases, each with a different burn rate from the aircraft's POH. If the diversion route involves a different altitude or power setting than the cruise, calculate that separately too. The fuel log in the flight plan must track fuel burn by sector so that EFR (Estimated Fuel Remaining) at each waypoint can be cross-checked in flight.
exam_tip: Fuel consumption planning: separate climb, cruise, descent (different burn rates). Add diversion fuel if diversion altitude/power differs. EFR must be trackable at each waypoint.
key_facts: Different fuel burn rates: climb > cruise > descent (typically)
Get burns from aircraft POH performance charts for each phase
Diversion fuel calculated separately if power/altitude differs from cruise
Track EFR at each waypoint to detect off-plan fuel trends early
Include contingency and reserve fuel separately in the plan
Convert all fuel quantities to consistent units (litres or kg, not both)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: For the preparation of a flight plan, determine: cruising level(s) considering topography, navigational and meteorological considerations
concept: Cruising level selection for IFR involves multiple constraints. The semi-circular (hemispheric) rule sets which levels are available for your track direction. MEA sets the minimum level on each airway. Topography may require higher levels near mountain ranges (e.g. Southern Alps). Meteorology affects: winds aloft (higher for tailwinds, or lower for headwinds if fuel is marginal), icing levels, turbulence, and the tops of cloud. RVSM airspace requirements kick in above FL290. Weight-limited aircraft may not be able to reach higher FLs. Balance all these to select the optimum level.
exam_tip: Cruising level selection: hemispheric rule + MEA + terrain + met (winds, icing, turbulence, cloud tops) + RVSM if above FL290 + aircraft capability.
key_facts: Hemispheric rule: determines available FL from the ENR 1.7 table
Must be at or above MEA on all airway segments
Terrain: Southern Alps require higher levels through that region
Winds: higher = stronger tailwinds generally (troposphere), but cold-soak on engine
Icing: avoid icing levels for non-de-iced aircraft
RVSM approval required above FL290
Aircraft weight and performance limits may cap achievable FL
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: For the preparation of a flight plan, determine: ATC and Noise Abatement requirements
concept: ATC requirements for IFR route selection include filing the flight plan in the ICAO standard format, obtaining airways clearances, complying with controlled airspace entry requirements, and meeting any mandatory filing times. Noise abatement requirements at certain NZ aerodromes restrict which SIDs can be used at night, or dictate specific power settings during departure. Check the relevant aerodrome section in AIPNZ for noise abatement procedures. Some departure routes are noise-preferred routes that must be followed.
exam_tip: ATC route requirements: file ICAO flight plan, comply with clearance requirements. Noise abatement: check aerodrome-specific procedures in AIPNZ -- may restrict SID choice, especially at night.
key_facts: File ICAO flight plan (form 2021 standard) with ATC
Clearance required prior to entering controlled airspace
Standard filing times: typically 30-60 min before EOBT for IFR
Noise abatement procedures published per aerodrome in AIPNZ
Noise-preferred routes may restrict available SIDs, especially at night
ATC may impose level or routing constraints for traffic management
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1, ICAO Doc 4444

syllabus_desc: For the preparation of a flight plan, determine: speed limitations, if applicable
concept: Speed limitations on IFR routes can come from several sources: ATC-imposed speed restrictions for sequencing, airspace speed limits (e.g. 250 kt IAS below FL100), SID and STAR speed constraints, and aircraft structural limits (VMO). Below FL100 in NZ, 250 kt IAS is the standard limit (Part 91). SID or STAR plates may publish lower speed constraints at specific fixes (e.g. 'not above 180 kt by OTAKI'). Turbine helicopters and slower piston aircraft may not be constrained by these limits but must still comply with any published procedure speeds.
exam_tip: Speed limits: 250 kt IAS below FL100 (CAR Part 91). Published SID/STAR constraints are mandatory. ATC may impose lower speeds for sequencing.
key_facts: 250 kt IAS below FL100: standard CAR Part 91 speed limit
SID speed constraints published on plates -- mandatory unless ATC amends
STAR speed constraints -- same: mandatory unless ATC amends
ATC may impose lower speeds for sequencing (e.g. '160 kt or less to final')
Aircraft VMO/MMO structural limits also apply
Turbine helicopters governed by their own speed limits
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91.209

syllabus_desc: For the preparation of a flight plan, determine: requirement for, and availability of, alternate(s)
concept: Alternate requirements for IFR apply when destination weather is uncertain or the aerodrome has limitations. CAR Part 91 specifies when an IFR alternate must be nominated (generally when the destination weather forecast is below required minima for the 1-hour ETA window, or the aerodrome is limited to one approach). Once you decide you need an alternate, check: (1) that the alternate has a published instrument approach, (2) the forecast weather at the alternate meets the published IFR alternate minima for ETA +/- 1 hour, and (3) that you have enough fuel to reach the alternate plus reserves.
exam_tip: Alternate required when: destination weather forecast below minima, or limited approach availability. Alternate must have: instrument approach, weather at or above alternate minima, fuel to reach it + reserves.
key_facts: CAR Part 91: specifies when IFR alternate is required
Alternate must have a published instrument approach
Forecast at alternate must meet IFR alternate minima for ETA +/- 1 hour
Fuel to alternate + reserve + contingency must be carried
Alternate minima published in AIPNZ Vol 3 alternate minima table
Multiple alternates may be required for remote/single-approach aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: point of departure including minimum departure altitude or departure instructions, if applicable
concept: The point of departure in an IFR navigation flight plan log is the ADEP with its ICAO indicator, followed by the minimum departure altitude (MDA) or the departure instructions from the published SID. If the SID specifies 'climb to 5,000 ft on runway heading before turning', the departure section records that requirement so the crew knows what to do from wheels-up. If no SID is applicable, the minimum safe altitude for the first sector is used as the departure altitude.
exam_tip: Departure: record ADEP (ICAO code) + minimum departure altitude or SID departure instructions. Sets the initial climb requirement.
key_facts: ADEP identified by 4-letter ICAO location indicator
Minimum departure altitude: from SID or, if no SID, from MSA calculation
SID departure instructions recorded (e.g. heading, turning point, initial altitude)
Defines what the crew does from wheels-up to joining en route
Minimum departure altitude ensures obstacle clearance from start of climb
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: rate of climb required to comply with published climb gradient
concept: If the published SID specifies a minimum climb gradient (e.g. 3.8% or 380 ft/nm), the flight plan log records the required rate of climb at your planned departure groundspeed. Convert the gradient to an ROC using: ROC (ft/min) = gradient (ft/nm) x GS (nm/min). For example, 380 ft/nm at 90 kt GS = 380 x 1.5 = 570 ft/min. If the aircraft can't achieve this ROC at the planned weight and conditions, the IFR departure cannot be conducted. This is a pre-flight calculation, not something to discover on the takeoff roll.
exam_tip: Required ROC from gradient: ROC (ft/min) = gradient (ft/nm) x GS (nm/min). Verify achievable pre-flight. Standard minimum is 200 ft/nm. Higher if terrain demands.
key_facts: Convert gradient: ROC (ft/min) = gradient (ft/nm) x GS (kt / 60)
200 ft/nm at 90 kt = 200 x 1.5 = 300 ft/min minimum
Higher gradients specified when terrain requires more than standard
Must confirm achievable at actual departure weight and conditions
Published on SID plate and in AIPNZ departure procedure text
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: location and altitude of top of climb and top of descent
concept: Top of Climb (TOC) is the position and altitude at which the aircraft levels off at cruise altitude. Top of Descent (TOD) is the position at which descent must begin to arrive at the first STAR altitude constraint or the initial approach fix at the correct altitude. Both are calculated from performance data (climb/descent rates at planned speeds) and charted on the navigation log as a distance from a waypoint. TOC and TOD must be checked against terrain on the chart -- in NZ's mountainous terrain, TOC and TOD positions relative to the Southern Alps can be critical.
exam_tip: TOC: position/altitude where cruise begins. TOD: position where descent starts to meet first STAR/approach altitude constraint. Both are calculated positions, not guesses.
key_facts: TOC: position and altitude at end of climb phase
TOD: position at which descent commences to meet first descent constraint
Calculated from climb/descent performance data and planned speeds
Recorded in navigation log as distance from a waypoint
Critical in mountainous terrain (NZ Southern Alps) -- TOC must clear terrain
TOD planned to meet first STAR altitude -- compute backwards from the fix
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: each sector of the flight identified as From/To
concept: Each sector of an IFR navigation log is identified as a From/To pair -- from one waypoint or fix to the next. This structures the log into discrete legs that can be tracked systematically. For example: NZCH (Christchurch) to REKIS, REKIS to BIDOL, BIDOL to NZWN (Wellington). Each From/To pair gets its own line in the nav log with track, distance, wind, TAS, heading, GS, time, and fuel. This structure allows the crew to update ETAs and EFRs incrementally and detect deviations from plan.
exam_tip: Sectors identified as From/To pairs (e.g. NZCH to REKIS). Each sector gets its own nav log line. Systematic tracking enables early deviation detection.
key_facts: Each sector: From waypoint / To waypoint
One nav log line per sector
Enables incremental ETA and EFR cross-checking
Waypoints may be named fixes, VOR/DME stations, lat/long positions
Includes climb, cruise, descent and diversion sectors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: point of arrival including minimum procedure commencement altitude, if applicable
concept: The point of arrival in the navigation log is the ADES (destination aerodrome) with its ICAO code, plus the minimum procedure commencement altitude -- the altitude at which the instrument approach procedure begins. For an ILS approach this might be the procedure altitude at the IAF or the glideslope intercept altitude. For a VOR approach it might be the altitude at the IAF. This altitude must be achievable on the STAR and must be noted in the nav log so the crew can verify they're on profile for the approach.
exam_tip: Arrival: record ADES (ICAO code) + minimum procedure commencement altitude. This is the altitude at the IAF or start of the approach procedure.
key_facts: ADES identified by 4-letter ICAO location indicator
Minimum procedure commencement altitude: altitude at IAF or procedure start
Ensures STAR delivers aircraft at the correct altitude to commence approach
Must be achievable from the final STAR segment
ILS: typically glideslope intercept altitude; VOR/NDB: IAF altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: the altitude and time of each sector including mean climb and mean descent altitude
concept: For each sector in the nav log, the altitude and time are recorded -- but for climb and descent sectors, a mean altitude is used (halfway between start and end altitude) for fuel and TAS calculations, since the aircraft spends equal time above and below the mean altitude. This approximation is standard practice and accurate enough for planning. The sector time is calculated from the sector distance and groundspeed. Record start altitude, end altitude, and mean altitude for each non-cruise sector.
exam_tip: Climb/descent sectors: use mean altitude for TAS and fuel calculations (halfway between start and end altitudes). Record start altitude, end altitude, and mean altitude in the nav log.
key_facts: Mean altitude = (start altitude + end altitude) / 2
Used for TAS calculation in climb/descent sectors (temperature varies with altitude)
Fuel burn rate from POH at mean altitude and mean OAT for that sector
Sector time from distance and mean groundspeed
Cruise sectors: single altitude used throughout
Split climb/descent: two sub-sectors if significant direction or level changes occur
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: each sector distance
concept: Each sector distance is the track distance (in nautical miles) from the From waypoint to the To waypoint. This is read from the en route chart alongside the airway or direct segment. For DME arcs, the arc distance must be calculated using arc length = (arc angle in degrees / 360) x 2 x pi x DME radius. Sector distances are the foundation of ETA and fuel calculations -- if they're wrong, everything else is wrong too. Cross-check against chart before committing to the plan.
exam_tip: Sector distance = track nm from From to To waypoint. Read from chart. DME arc: arc length = (angle/360) x 2 x pi x radius. Accuracy critical for ETAs and fuel.
key_facts: Track distance in nautical miles, read from chart
DME arc sector: arc length = (arc angle / 360) x 2pi x DME radius
All distances are track distances (not great circle for shorter segments)
Source: en route chart (distance printed alongside airway segment)
Foundation of ETA and fuel calculations -- errors propagate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: outside air temperatures for the calculation of TAS during climb, cruise and descent
concept: Outside air temperature (OAT) is used to calculate TAS from IAS (via CAS and EAS), and to determine fuel burn from the performance charts. OAT is obtained from the forecast temperature at the planned cruising altitude (from the TAF, SIGMET, upper wind and temperature forecasts). For each sector, note the OAT at the mean altitude. In New Zealand, upper winds and temperatures are published on GRAFOR charts and available from MetService. Temperature also affects density altitude and engine performance.
exam_tip: OAT for each sector: from upper wind and temperature forecasts (MetService GRAFOR or similar). Used for TAS calculation and fuel burn from POH performance charts.
key_facts: OAT from forecast upper wind and temperature charts (e.g. MetService GRAFOR)
Used to convert IAS to TAS via density/pressure altitude
Used to determine fuel burn from POH performance charts
Note OAT at mean altitude for climb/descent sectors
OAT also affects engine power output and density altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: the wind velocity used for climb, cruise and descent, including split climb and split descent
concept: Wind velocity used for planning is the forecast wind at the planned cruising altitude, from a synoptic or upper-air forecast. In NZ, this comes from MetService upper wind and temperature forecasts (GRAPHORECASTor GRAFOR). For climb and descent sectors, use a mean wind between surface and cruising altitude, or split into sub-sectors. A 'split climb' or 'split descent' means the climb or descent crosses a significant wind shift, requiring the sector to be divided at the wind-shift altitude to use an appropriate wind for each sub-sector.
exam_tip: Wind velocity from upper wind forecast (GRAFOR etc.) at cruise altitude. Split climb/descent: divide sector at significant wind shifts for accurate heading and ETA calculations.
key_facts: Wind from MetService upper wind forecast (GRAFOR or similar)
Applied at planned cruising altitude for cruise sectors
Climb/descent: use mean wind or split the sector at wind shifts
Split climb: divide at altitude where wind changes significantly
Wind is applied in the triangle of velocity to calculate heading and GS
Always note wind as direction/speed (e.g. 270/35)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: TAS for each sector
concept: True airspeed (TAS) is calculated from calibrated airspeed (CAS) corrected for density altitude using the navigation computer or performance charts. For IFR planning, TAS varies with altitude and OAT -- higher altitude and lower temperature give higher TAS for the same IAS/CAS. TAS is the input to the triangle of velocity along with the wind vector to give heading and groundspeed. Record TAS for each sector in the nav log -- it changes between climb, cruise, and descent if different power settings or altitudes are used.
exam_tip: TAS varies by sector (altitude and temperature differ). Calculate from CAS using nav computer or POH. TAS is input to triangle of velocity for heading and GS.
key_facts: TAS = CAS corrected for density altitude
Higher altitude and lower temperature: higher TAS for same IAS/CAS
Use navigation computer (CR3 or similar) or POH performance charts
TAS varies: climb TAS differs from cruise TAS
TAS + wind vector = triangle of velocity = heading + GS
Record in nav log for each sector
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: track (degrees M) of each sector
concept: The magnetic track for each sector is read from the en route chart alongside the airway segment (in degrees M). For direct (off-airways) routing, the true track is calculated from the coordinates and then magnetic variation is applied to get magnetic track. In New Zealand, magnetic variation varies around the country -- check the isogonic lines on the chart or the AIPNZ for local variation. Magnetic track is not the same as heading -- it's the intended direction of travel over the ground.
exam_tip: Magnetic track = track read from chart for your direction of travel (degrees M). For off-airways: calculate true track from coordinates then apply magnetic variation.
key_facts: Magnetic track (degrees M): read from en route chart alongside airway
For direct routing: calculate true track from coordinates, apply variation
NZ magnetic variation varies regionally -- check isogonals on chart
Magnetic track is direction of travel over ground, not heading through air
Track + wind correction = magnetic heading to fly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: heading (degrees M), groundspeed and time for each sector
concept: The magnetic heading (HDG) is the course to steer, derived from the triangle of velocity: TAS + wind vector = groundspeed and wind correction angle (WCA). Apply WCA to the magnetic track to get magnetic heading. Groundspeed (GS) is then used to calculate sector time (time = distance / GS). Record heading, GS, and time for each sector. In the flight deck, the heading to fly is set on the HI or DI; the groundspeed can be cross-checked against DME if available.
exam_tip: Magnetic heading = track +/- wind correction angle. GS from triangle of velocity. Time = distance / GS. Record all three in nav log for each sector.
key_facts: HDG = track +/- WCA (from triangle of velocity)
GS derived from TAS and wind vector
Time per sector = distance (nm) / GS (kt) x 60 = minutes
Record HDG, GS, and time for each From/To sector
Cross-check GS in flight using DME time over a known distance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Prepare an IFR flight plan which contains: climb, cruise and descent details of a diversion
concept: Diversion planning in the IFR nav log mirrors the main flight plan but for the route from destination to the nominated alternate. Record the diversion track, TAS (may differ if diversion is at a different altitude or power setting), wind velocity (may differ if diverting at a lower altitude after a missed approach), heading, GS, distance, and sector time. Also calculate the fuel required for the diversion. All of this is pre-computed in the nav log so that, in the event of a missed approach at destination, the crew can immediately turn to the diversion sector and know exactly what to fly and how much fuel remains.
exam_tip: Diversion in the nav log: same elements as main flight (track, TAS, wind, HDG, GS, distance, time, fuel) from destination to alternate. Pre-computed so no mental arithmetic needed in the cockpit during a missed approach.
key_facts: Diversion sector: ADES to alternate, with all nav log elements calculated
TAS may differ: diversion often at lower altitude = lower TAS
Wind may differ: lower altitude post-missed-approach = different wind
Pre-compute diversion fuel: track fuel used, know fuel available for diversion
Enables immediate execution of diversion without in-cockpit calculation
Also include fuel for holding at destination before diverting if applicable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Calculate total fuel load required including provision for diversion, reserve and contingency fuel
concept: IFR fuel planning requires calculating the total fuel to carry, which must cover four components: (1) trip fuel -- the fuel to fly from ADEP to ADES at the planned route and altitude; (2) diversion fuel -- fuel to fly from ADES to the nominated alternate, including the missed approach at destination; (3) contingency fuel -- extra fuel for uncertainties (typically 5% of trip fuel or a minimum amount per Part 91); and (4) reserve fuel -- the final fuel on landing at the alternate that must not be below the required minimum (typically 45 minutes at holding speed for piston, 30 minutes for turbine under Part 91). Total fuel = trip + diversion + contingency + reserve. If taxi fuel is significant (long taxi at a complex aerodrome), add that too. The fuel figure is then cross-checked against the aircraft's usable fuel capacity -- if it doesn't fit, the route, altitude, or alternate must change.
exam_tip: IFR total fuel = trip + diversion + contingency (5% min) + reserve (45 min piston / 30 min turbine under NZ Part 91). Calculate each component separately. Always cross-check against usable fuel capacity.
key_facts: Trip fuel: ADEP to ADES at planned route/level
Diversion fuel: ADES to alternate (include missed approach fuel if significant)
Contingency fuel: 5% of trip fuel minimum (or as required by operator)
Reserve fuel: 45 min holding for piston / 30 min for turbine (check CAR Part 91 for current figures)
Total fuel must not exceed usable fuel capacity
Add taxi fuel if applicable
Fuel plan drives alternate selection -- if no alternate with enough fuel, plan must change
Cross-check EFR at each waypoint during flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Define: drift, drift angle, drift correction
concept: Drift is the sideways displacement of an aircraft from its intended track caused by a crosswind component. Drift angle is the angle between the aircraft's heading (direction the nose points) and the track actually made good over the ground. If you're heading 090 degrees but tracking 095 degrees, your drift is 5 degrees to the right (starboard drift). Drift correction (also called wind correction angle, WCA) is the number of degrees you offset your heading into the wind to counteract drift and fly your intended track. Point the nose upwind of the track by the drift angle.
exam_tip: Drift = sideways displacement. Drift angle = angle between heading and track made good. Drift correction (WCA) = degrees to offset heading into wind to maintain track.
key_facts: Drift: aircraft displaced sideways from track by crosswind
Drift angle: angle between heading and track made good
Port drift: wind from left pushes aircraft right of track
Starboard drift: wind from right pushes aircraft left of track
Drift correction (WCA): offset heading into wind by drift angle to maintain track
Drift angle and WCA are numerically equal but WCA is applied to heading
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Define: track error, closing angle, total correction
concept: Track error (TE) is the angular difference between the required track and the track you're actually making. If you should be tracking 270 but your actual track over the ground is 275, you have a 5-degree track error to the right (right of track). Closing angle (CA) is the additional angle you need to apply beyond the track correction to re-intercept the required track within a specified distance (often at the next waypoint). Total correction (TC) = TE + CA. So if TE is 5 degrees and you want to close back to track in the same distance you've already flown, CA is also 5 degrees, making TC = 10 degrees to the left.
exam_tip: TE = angular difference between required track and actual track. CA = extra angle to close back to track by a specified point. TC = TE + CA.
key_facts: Track Error (TE): angle between required track and actual track
Left of track: TE is to the left; right of track: TE is to the right
Closing Angle (CA): additional angle to return to track by a specific point
Total Correction (TC) = TE + CA
Apply TC to current heading to regain track
Using 1 in 60 rule: TE (degrees) = distance off track (nm) / distance flown (nm) x 60
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Define: magnetic and true bearing
concept: A magnetic bearing is the direction FROM or TO a point measured in degrees relative to magnetic north. A true bearing uses geographic (true) north as the reference. The difference is magnetic variation (easterly or westerly). In NZ, variation is predominantly easterly (the compass points west of true north), so to convert true to magnetic, you subtract easterly variation (or 'east is least' -- subtract the variation). To convert magnetic to true, add easterly variation ('west is best' -- add). For example, with 20 degrees east variation: true 090 = magnetic 070.
exam_tip: 'East is least, west is best': subtract easterly variation to convert true to magnetic; add easterly variation to convert magnetic to true. NZ variation is predominantly easterly.
key_facts: Magnetic bearing: direction measured from magnetic north
True bearing: direction measured from true (geographic) north
Magnetic variation: angle between magnetic and true north
East variation: magnetic north is west of true north -- subtract from true to get magnetic
'East is least' (subtract), 'West is best' (add)
NZ: predominantly easterly variation (varies 15-25 degrees east regionally)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Define: position line
concept: A position line (PL) is a line along which the aircraft is known to be situated. A single nav aid gives you one position line -- you know you're on that radial, or at that bearing, or at that distance -- but you don't know exactly where on that line you are. A VOR gives you a radial (a position line). A DME gives you a distance circle (another type of position line). A single NDB gives you a bearing line. You need at least two position lines to establish a fix.
exam_tip: Position line = line of known position (radial, bearing, DME arc). One PL alone is insufficient -- need minimum two PLs to fix your position.
key_facts: A line along which the aircraft is known to be positioned
VOR: provides a radial (straight position line through the station)
DME: provides a range arc (circular position line centred on station)
NDB: provides a bearing line
Single position line: ambiguous -- aircraft is somewhere on that line
Two intersecting position lines = a fix
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Define: fix
concept: A fix is a positively identified position established by the intersection of two or more position lines. The more position lines converging, the more accurate the fix. VOR + DME gives an excellent fix (radial + range = single point). VOR + VOR gives a cross-radial fix. NDB bearing + NDB bearing is a less accurate but acceptable fix in the absence of better options. GNSS gives an automatic fix from satellite geometry. In IFR navigation, fixes are taken at reporting points, before descent, and on approach to verify position.
exam_tip: Fix = intersection of two or more position lines. VOR+DME = highly accurate (single point). VOR+VOR = cross-radial fix. More PLs = more accurate. GNSS automatically provides continuous fixes.
key_facts: A definite known position established by two or more position lines
VOR + DME: most accurate (radial + range defines a unique point)
VOR + VOR: cross-radial fix (two straight PLs intersecting)
NDB bearing + NDB bearing: acceptable but less precise
GNSS: continuous automatic position fix
Fix accuracy depends on cut angle (90 degrees ideal; less than 30 degrees poor)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Describe the principles involved in obtaining an accurate fix
concept: The principles for an accurate fix include: (1) Use two or more position lines that cross at close to 90 degrees -- a shallow cut angle produces a large error zone; (2) Take simultaneous observations where possible -- sequential bearings introduce time errors; (3) Use the most accurate nav sources available (DME is more accurate than VOR bearing for range, GNSS is the most accurate); (4) Cross-check any fix against your expected position from dead reckoning -- a wildly unexpected fix suggests an error, not a miracle; (5) Position lines with significant time separation should be corrected for track and distance flown ('running fix').
exam_tip: Accurate fix principles: cut angle close to 90 degrees, simultaneous observations, best available sources, cross-check against DR, correct sequential observations for elapsed movement (running fix).
key_facts: Cut angle: ideally 90 degrees; <30 degrees = poor accuracy
Simultaneous observations eliminate time error
Best available sources: DME > VOR bearing for range; GNSS = highest accuracy
Cross-check against DR: unexpected fix may indicate observation error
Running fix: correct sequential observations for track and distance flown between them
More position lines = more confidence in fix
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Solve problems involving: the triangle of velocity
concept: The triangle of velocity is the vector diagram that relates true airspeed (TAS), wind velocity (W/V), and groundspeed (GS). TAS is the air vector (how fast and which way the aircraft moves through the air mass). W/V is the wind vector (how fast and which way the air mass moves over the ground). GS is the ground vector (the resultant -- how fast and which way the aircraft actually moves over the ground). Solve the triangle to find any unknown: heading to fly (for a given track and W/V), GS, or drift angle. The navigation computer (CR3 or E6B) solves this graphically.
exam_tip: Triangle of velocity: TAS vector + W/V vector = GS vector. Use the nav computer to solve for heading, GS, or drift. Components: air vector (TAS + heading), wind vector, ground vector (track + GS).
key_facts: Three vectors: air vector (TAS + heading), wind vector (W/V), ground vector (track + GS)
Any one unknown can be solved if the other two are known
Navigation computer (CR-3/E6B) solves graphically
Drift angle (WCA) = angle between heading and track
Groundspeed affects sector times and fuel consumption
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Solve problems involving: the 1 in 60 rule
concept: The 1 in 60 rule states that 1 degree of angle subtends approximately 1 nautical mile at a distance of 60 nm. This is used for mental navigation calculations. Track Error: TE (degrees) = distance off track (nm) x 60 / distance flown (nm). Closing angle: CA (degrees) = distance off track (nm) x 60 / distance to go (nm). So if you're 3 nm off track after flying 60 nm, your TE = 3 x 60/60 = 3 degrees. If you have 60 nm to go, your CA = 3 x 60/60 = 3 degrees, and TC = 6 degrees.
exam_tip: 1 in 60 rule: 1 degree = 1 nm at 60 nm. TE = (off track nm x 60) / distance flown nm. CA = (off track nm x 60) / distance to go nm. TC = TE + CA.
key_facts: 1 degree subtends approximately 1 nm at 60 nm range
TE (degrees) = (cross-track distance nm x 60) / distance flown nm
CA (degrees) = (cross-track distance nm x 60) / distance to go nm
TC = TE + CA (apply to heading in correct direction)
Quick mental maths tool -- accurate enough for airborne corrections
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Solve problems involving: time/speed/distance
concept: Time, speed, and distance problems are the bread and butter of IFR navigation. The relationships: Distance = Speed x Time; Speed = Distance / Time; Time = Distance / Speed. Use consistent units -- if speed is in knots (nm/hr) and distance in nm, time comes out in hours; multiply by 60 for minutes. The navigation computer handles this directly on the inner/outer scales. Common application: given groundspeed and remaining distance, calculate ETA. Or given time flown and groundspeed, calculate distance covered.
exam_tip: D = S x T; S = D/T; T = D/S. Speed in kt, distance in nm, time in hours (x60 for minutes). Nav computer solves directly. Cross-check ETA at each waypoint in flight.
key_facts: D = S x T (all consistent units)
Speed in knots x time in hours = distance in nm
Time (min) = distance (nm) / GS (kt) x 60
Nav computer: align speed over 60 on inner/outer scales for direct read-off
ETA = departure time + sector times
Update ETA whenever GS changes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Solve problems involving: time/fuel used/fuel consumption rate
concept: Fuel problems follow the same structure: Fuel used = flow rate x time; flow rate = fuel used / time; time = fuel used / flow rate. Fuel flow is typically in litres per hour or kg/hr from the POH. Common problems: how long before fuel exhaustion (time = fuel remaining / flow rate), or how much fuel used in a sector (flow rate x sector time). Endurance is total usable fuel divided by fuel flow rate -- how long you can fly. Cross-check EFR against planned at every waypoint.
exam_tip: Fuel used = flow rate x time. Endurance = usable fuel / flow rate. Fuel remaining at waypoint = fuel on board at departure minus fuel burned to that point. Cross-check EFR in flight.
key_facts: Fuel used = flow rate x time (consistent units -- litres/hr x hours = litres)
Endurance = total usable fuel / fuel flow rate
EFR (Estimated Fuel Remaining) = initial fuel - fuel burned
Compare EFR against planned at each waypoint
If EFR is below planned: investigate -- headwind, higher than planned power
Fuel flow changes with altitude, power, and mixture setting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Solve problems involving: height/time/distance/rate of climb/rate of descent
concept: Height, time, distance, and rate of climb/descent calculations link the vertical and horizontal components of a climb or descent. If you need to descend 6,000 ft at a groundspeed of 180 kt and your aircraft descends at 1,000 ft/min, the time to descend = 6,000/1,000 = 6 minutes. Distance covered = 180 kt x (6/60) hr = 18 nm. So your TOD must be 18 nm before the target altitude. Going the other way: if you must lose 6,000 ft in 18 nm at 180 kt, your required rate of descent = 6,000 ft / (18 nm / 180 kt) = 6,000 / 0.1 hr = 60,000 ft/hr = 1,000 ft/min.
exam_tip: Distance to descend (nm) = GS (kt) x descent time (hr). Required ROD (ft/min) = altitude to lose / time of descent. Time = distance / GS; altitude loss = ROD x time.
key_facts: Descent time (hr) = altitude loss (ft) / ROD (ft/hr = ft/min x 60)
Distance (nm) = GS (kt) x descent time (hr)
Required ROD (ft/min) = altitude loss x GS / distance x (1/60)
For 3-degree path: ROD (ft/min) approximately GS (kt) x 5
TOD distance from target = distance covered during descent
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Solve problems involving: calculate the track miles flown on a segment of a DME arc
concept: To calculate track miles flown on a segment of a DME arc, you need the arc angle flown (in degrees) and the arc radius (in nm). Arc length = (arc angle / 360) x 2 x pi x radius. Simplified: arc length (nm) = arc angle (degrees) x pi x radius / 180. For example, a 90-degree arc at 10 DME = (90/360) x 2 x pi x 10 = 0.25 x 62.83 = 15.7 nm. This tells you how many track miles you've flown on the arc, which is used for fuel calculations and ETA estimates while flying the arc.
exam_tip: DME arc track miles = (arc angle degrees / 360) x 2 x pi x radius nm. Or: arc angle x pi x radius / 180. Know this formula for the exam.
key_facts: Arc length (nm) = (arc angle / 360) x 2 x pi x DME radius
Simplified: arc length = arc angle x pi x radius / 180
Example: 90-degree arc at 10 DME = 15.7 nm
Used for fuel consumption on the arc and ETA at end of arc
Distance steps on the arc chart help track position during the arc
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: describe navigation aspects associated with published departure procedures
concept: Published departure procedures (SIDs) define the initial routing and climb profile for IFR departures. When navigating a SID using VOR/DME, you track outbound radials, intercept crossing radials to turn, and use DME to identify turning points and step-climb altitudes. With GNSS, the FMS sequences through the published SID waypoints automatically. Either way, cross-check the published ground track against displayed CDI/HSI/map to ensure you're on the correct lateral path. Any altitude crossing requirements on the SID must be met -- use vertical speed to control the profile.
exam_tip: Departure procedure navigation: VOR track outbound radials and DME turn points; GNSS sequences waypoints via FMS. Cross-check lateral path on CDI/HSI. Meet altitude crossing requirements.
key_facts: VOR/DME: track specified radials outbound, use DME for turn/altitude trigger points
GNSS: FMS sequences through SID waypoints if programmed correctly
Verify lateral path on CDI or HSI against chart depiction
All altitude crossing requirements on the SID are mandatory
After SID terminus: join en route structure as cleared
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: calculate magnetic headings required to maintain or regain required magnetic tracks
concept: To calculate the magnetic heading to maintain or regain a required magnetic track using VOR/DME information, use the triangle of velocity: apply the known wind velocity and TAS to the required track to calculate the wind correction angle. Heading = Track +/- WCA. Drift correction into the wind: if wind is from the right, add WCA to track; if wind is from the left, subtract WCA. To regain a track after drift: apply additional correction (total correction = TE + CA). With VOR, the CDI shows your lateral displacement and helps you confirm when you're back on track.
exam_tip: Heading = track +/- WCA. Wind from right: add WCA to track. Wind from left: subtract. To regain track: apply total correction (TE + CA). Confirm on CDI when back on track.
key_facts: HDG = track + WCA (wind from right) or track - WCA (wind from left)
WCA calculated using triangle of velocity (nav computer)
To regain track: HDG correction = TE + CA
VOR CDI confirms lateral position on the radial/track
DME confirms along-track position
Update WCA if wind changes significantly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: determine a position in relation to a navigation aid or aids
concept: Determining position relative to a navigation aid using VOR/DME: the VOR gives you the radial you're on (bearing from the station), and the DME gives you slant range. Together, VOR radial + DME distance places you at a unique point -- on the specific radial at the specific distance from the VOR/DME. With two VORs, two radials give a cross-fix. With GNSS, position is continuous and automatic. To determine position: tune the VOR, centre the OBS, read the radial (TO or FROM). Combine with DME for precise fix.
exam_tip: VOR + DME: radial (FROM reading) + DME distance = unique position fix. Two VORs: cross-radial fix. GNSS: automatic continuous fix.
key_facts: VOR radial (FROM reading): bearing FROM the station to aircraft
DME: slant range -- uniquely defines position on that radial
VOR alone: position line (one of many points on that radial)
VOR + DME: definite fix
Cross-VOR fix: two VOR radials intersect at one point
GNSS: automatic position independent of ground-based navaids
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: calculate magnetic tracks to specified point(s)
concept: To calculate magnetic tracks to specified points using VOR/DME or GNSS: from your current position, draw or compute the bearing to the target point. If using VOR, tune the target VOR, rotate the OBS until you get a TO indication and zero CDI -- the OBS reading gives the magnetic track to the station (FROM aircraft). From GNSS: the FMS or CDI displays desired track to the active waypoint directly. For non-navaid waypoints, calculate the true track from coordinates (using the nav computer or mental geometry) then apply magnetic variation.
exam_tip: VOR track to station: rotate OBS to zero CDI with TO indication -- OBS reading = magnetic track to station. GNSS: reads track to waypoint directly on FMS/CDI display.
key_facts: VOR TO indication + zero CDI: OBS reading = magnetic track from aircraft to station
VOR FROM indication: OBS reading = magnetic track away from station
GNSS FMS: displays desired track to active waypoint directly
For non-navaid waypoints: true track from coordinates + magnetic variation = magnetic track
Magnetic track to a point = reciprocal of the bearing FROM that point
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: calculate groundspeed
concept: Groundspeed can be calculated using DME by timing over a known DME distance: GS (kt) = distance (nm) / time (hr). For example, if you cover 10 nm of DME in 4 minutes, GS = 10 / (4/60) = 10 x 15 = 150 kt. With GNSS, groundspeed is displayed continuously and directly. With two position fixes at known time intervals, GS = distance between fixes / time elapsed. Accurate GS is essential for updating ETAs and checking fuel consumption against plan.
exam_tip: GS from DME: GS (kt) = nm / (time in min / 60). GNSS shows GS directly. Always update ETA and EFR when GS differs from planned.
key_facts: GS (kt) = distance (nm) / time (hours) = nm / (min/60)
DME method: time over a known DME distance
GNSS: continuous direct readout
GS differs from TAS by the headwind or tailwind component
Update ETA if actual GS differs significantly from planned GS
GS also used to calculate required ROD for 3-degree approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: calculate estimated times of arrival at destination or intermediate positions
concept: Estimated time of arrival (ETA) at destination or intermediate positions: ETA = departure time + sum of sector times. Update the ETA continuously in flight as GS deviates from plan. ETA updates should be passed to ATC whenever the ETA changes by 3 minutes or more. For intermediate positions, calculate ETA = departure time + time elapsed to that waypoint. GNSS and FMS calculate and display ETAs automatically. Always cross-check FMS ETAs against manual estimates, especially after reprogramming.
exam_tip: ETA = departure time + accumulated sector times. Advise ATC if ETA changes by 3 minutes or more. GNSS/FMS displays ETA but always cross-check after any reprogramming.
key_facts: ETA = departure time + sum of sector times to that point
Update when actual GS differs from planned
Report revised ETA to ATC if change exceeds 3 minutes
GNSS/FMS: automatic ETA display -- verify after any route changes
ETA errors compound over long sectors if GS check is delayed
ETA at destination used for alternate fuel calculation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: determine requirements with respect TOC/TOD and rate of climb/rate of descent
concept: Top of Climb (TOC) and Top of Descent (TOD) requirements can be derived from VOR/DME information in flight. TOC: monitor DME distance from a navaid -- when you expect to reach cruise altitude, note the DME distance and check it against plan. TOD: work out when to begin descent so that you arrive at the first STAR altitude constraint at the right level. With the 3-degree rule: distance to TOD (nm) = altitude to lose (ft) / (GS x 5 / GS) -- simplified: altitude to lose / 300 per nm at typical speeds. With GNSS/FMS: TOD is computed and displayed automatically.
exam_tip: TOD distance from target altitude: altitude to lose (ft) divided by ROD (ft/nm). For 3-degree path: roughly altitude loss / 300 per nm at 100 kt, adjust for GS. FMS computes automatically.
key_facts: TOC: altitude at which cruise begins -- track against DME or waypoint
TOD: point where descent starts to meet first STAR crossing altitude
3-degree descent path: ROD ft/nm = GS (kt) x 5 / 60 x 60 -- simplified to ~300 ft/nm at 100 kt
Distance to descend = altitude to lose / ROD per nm
FMS computes TOD automatically based on arrival constraints
Always cross-check FMS TOD against mental estimate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: position in terms of a radial and distance to or from a navigation aid
concept: Position in terms of a radial and distance from a navigation aid is the standard IFR position reporting format. Format: '[aircraft] is [radial] radial, [distance] DME from [navaid]'. For example: 'ZK-ABC, Hamilton 085 radial, 45 DME'. The radial is the VOR bearing FROM the station to the aircraft (OBS set to TO, then add 180 for FROM radial, or just read the FROM side). DME gives the slant range. Position can also be stated as: '[distance] on the [radial]' or as a waypoint name.
exam_tip: Position stated as: radial from the station and DME distance. Format: '[station] [xxx] radial, [nn] DME.' The radial is FROM the station.
key_facts: Standard IFR position format: [station name] [radial] radial, [DME] DME
Radial is FROM the station (the bearing FROM the station TO the aircraft)
DME gives range (slant range -- slightly more than actual distance at low altitudes)
Can be cross-checked by tuning the relevant VOR and reading OBS with TO/FROM
Position can be reported as waypoint name when at a defined fix
Required at compulsory reporting points without ATC prompting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ ENR 1.1

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: calculate fuel consumption, and operational details or requirements resulting from fuel flow information
concept: Fuel consumption monitoring in flight: compare actual fuel flow against the planned flow from the nav log. Calculate EFR at each waypoint (initial fuel minus fuel burned to that point). If EFR is below plan, you're burning more than expected -- investigate (higher power? stronger headwind? fuel mismanagement?). If significantly below plan at a critical fuel point, you may need to reconsider the alternate or direct-route to destination. Fuel flow instruments give actual consumption; compare to the POH values used in planning. Don't forget to cross-check fuel tank contents against EFR -- particularly important if one tank is running out faster than the other.
exam_tip: Monitor EFR at each waypoint. EFR = fuel loaded - fuel burned. Compare to planned EFR. If below plan: investigate immediately. Cross-check gauges against calculated EFR.
key_facts: EFR (Estimated Fuel Remaining) = initial fuel - fuel burned to this point
Compare EFR to planned EFR at each waypoint
Below-plan EFR: higher burn rate (higher power, headwind, fuel system issue)
Critical fuel point: if EFR drops below alternate + reserve, take action
Cross-check fuel gauges against calculated EFR -- gauges can be inaccurate
Fuel imbalance: check tank selectors and cross-feeding if one tank depletes faster
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: describe navigation aspects associated with published arrival procedures
concept: Navigation aspects of published arrival procedures (STARs) using VOR/DME and GNSS: STARs are defined by waypoints, radials, and DME distances. On a VOR/DME STAR, you track outbound or inbound radials and use DME to identify waypoints. On a GNSS STAR, the FMS sequences through stored waypoints. Either way, verify the lateral path on the CDI/HSI against the plate. Altitude constraints are mandatory. Speed constraints are mandatory unless ATC amends. Position reports may be required at compulsory reporting points on the STAR.
exam_tip: STAR navigation: VOR radials + DME distance = waypoints. GNSS: FMS sequences. Verify lateral path on CDI/HSI. All altitude and speed constraints mandatory (unless ATC amends).
key_facts: VOR/DME STAR: track inbound/outbound radials, use DME for waypoint identification
GNSS STAR: FMS sequences through stored waypoints automatically
Cross-check lateral path on CDI/HSI/map display against plate
Altitude constraints mandatory -- comply with 'at', 'at or above', 'at or below'
Speed constraints mandatory unless ATC amends
Position reports at compulsory reporting points on STAR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: calculate holding time over a navigation aid before diversion must be commenced
concept: Holding time before diversion commences is calculated from the fuel remaining. Holding time available = (EFR at holding fix) minus (diversion fuel + reserve fuel) divided by holding fuel flow. Alternatively: max holding time = EFR - (fuel to alternate + fuel for approach at alternate + reserve) / holding fuel flow. Before entering the hold, calculate the fuel at which you must leave to reach the alternate with required reserves. Then monitor time in the hold and fuel -- when the threshold is reached, depart the hold and divert.
exam_tip: Max holding time = (EFR - diversion fuel - reserve fuel) / holding fuel flow. Calculate before entering hold. Depart the hold when fuel reaches the decision threshold.
key_facts: Calculate fuel threshold: diversion fuel + alternate approach fuel + reserve
Max holding time = (EFR - fuel threshold) / holding fuel flow rate
Declare fuel state to ATC if nearing minimum diversion fuel
Monitor time and fuel continuously in the hold
Depart hold when fuel reaches threshold -- not later
Holding fuel flow is typically slightly higher than cruise (lower altitude, circuit speeds)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: describe Distance steps
concept: Distance steps are stepped minimum altitudes based on DME distance from a reference navaid, as defined in AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.5-8.8.9. They are used for both REVERSE DME STEP departure procedures (climb-on-track with a progressive minimum altitude profile) and for enroute descent steps on arrival. At each stated DME value the aircraft must be at or above the published step altitude. Distance steps are NOT cumulative track miles for waypoint identification -- that is a different chart feature. When flying them, the DME readout from the reference facility is the primary reference; per AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.8, GNSS may not be substituted unless the pilot has a displayed distance from the reference point or facility.
exam_tip: AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.5-8.8.9: Distance steps are stepped minimum altitudes tied to DME from a reference navaid. REVERSE DME STEP on departure; enroute descent steps on arrival. GNSS NOT permitted as a substitute unless the pilot has a distance-from-reference readout.
key_facts: Stepped minimum altitudes based on DME from a reference navaid
REVERSE DME STEP procedure for climb-on-track departures (e.g. Gisborne H328)
Enroute descent steps on arrival: progressive minimum altitudes as DME reduces
DME from the reference facility is the primary reference instrument
GNSS substitution restriction: only permitted if pilot has distance from reference point (§8.8.8)
If DME fails on a REVERSE DME STEP departure, return toward the departure aid until reaching MSA and advise ATC (§8.8.7)
NOT cumulative track miles between waypoints (a different chart feature)
source: AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.5-8.8.9; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Corrected per AIP NZ authoritative source after courseware audit 2026-04-09.

syllabus_desc: Based on information derived from VOR and DME displays, and from GNSS instrumentation: describe a DME arc procedure
concept: A DME arc procedure is an approach segment (or part of a STAR) flown at a constant DME distance from a collocated VOR/DME or standalone DME. To fly it: maintain the published DME distance (e.g. 10 DME) using small heading changes to keep the DME constant. Lead the turn when the aircraft is within approximately 0.5 nm of the arc. Use the VOR radials as position checks around the arc (distance steps). When the arc intersects the inbound track radial, lead the turn inbound using approximately half the bank-in angle as a lead radial. The 'lead radial' is published on the plate to help time the inbound turn.
exam_tip: DME arc: fly at constant DME distance using small heading changes. Lead inbound turn using published lead radial. Use VOR radials as position checks around the arc.
key_facts: Maintain constant DME distance using small heading corrections
Lead the initial roll-onto-arc by approximately 0.5 nm
VOR radials = position checks around the arc (distance steps)
Lead radial published on plate: start inbound turn when crossing the lead radial
Arc track miles: (arc angle/360) x 2 x pi x radius
DME arcs typically used on VOR/DME approach procedures to intercept final approach course
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AIP NZ Vol 3

syllabus_desc: Calculate TOC and TOD for multi-leg climb and multi-leg descent profiles
concept: On a flight plan where the climb crosses more than one leg, the TOC is determined by working forward leg by leg: compute the climb distance for leg 1 at its mean wind and mean climb altitude; if the remaining climb distance exceeds leg 1, subtract leg 1 and continue on leg 2, recomputing mean climb altitude and wind for the next segment. TOD is calculated the same way in reverse -- start from ADES at the initial approach / minimum procedure commencement altitude, work backwards along the route using the mean descent altitude (cruise + base)/2 and the forecast wind for the descent segment. If the descent spans two legs, compute the descent distance on the segment closer to ADES first, then continue the remaining descent distance on the preceding leg with its own mean wind and mean altitude. The flight log must show the split point, the altitudes, the wind used, and the revised heading/GS/time for the sub-segments.
exam_tip: Multi-leg climb: compute leg 1 climb distance; if climb not complete, carry remaining climb onto leg 2 with its own mean altitude and wind. Multi-leg descent: same in reverse from ADES. Show the split point, altitude, wind and revised HDG/GS/TIME on the log.
key_facts: Mean climb altitude per segment = 2/3 (cruise - base) + base, rounded to nearest 500 ft
Mean descent altitude per segment = 1/2 (cruise - base) + base, rounded to nearest 500 ft
If climb not complete at end of leg 1, carry remainder onto leg 2 with new mean alt/wind
Descent is computed backwards from ADES, using descent mean altitude and wind
Show split point, altitudes, wind and revised HDG/GS/TIME for each sub-segment
Exam tolerances: +-2 kts TAS, +-2 deg HDG, +-2 min time, +-5 kg fuel
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.5
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: Determine the mean altitude for climb and descent wind/TAS calculations
concept: For wind and TAS interpolation during climb and descent, the mean altitude is used rather than the cruise altitude. The convention on NZ IFR exams is: mean climb altitude = 2/3 (cruise - base) + base, and mean descent altitude = 1/2 (cruise - base) + base. Both are rounded to the nearest 500 ft before being used to enter the area forecast wind table. The reasoning: climb spends proportionally longer at higher altitudes (accelerating into cruise), so 2/3 weighting; descent spends proportionally longer at lower altitudes, so 1/2 weighting. These exact fractions are the values expected in the ASPEQ answer scheme for IFR NAV flight log calculations.
exam_tip: Mean climb altitude = 2/3 (cruise - base) + base. Mean descent altitude = 1/2 (cruise - base) + base. Round to nearest 500 ft before pulling wind and temperature.
key_facts: Climb: mean alt = 2/3 * (cruise - base) + base, rounded to nearest 500 ft
Descent: mean alt = 1/2 * (cruise - base) + base, rounded to nearest 500 ft
Used for area-forecast wind and temperature interpolation (SFC / 5000 / 10000 / 15000 / 20000)
Feeds directly into TAS and heading/GS/time calculations for the climb/descent segment
Do NOT use cruise altitude for climb/descent wind -- that overestimates wind in the climb and descent phase
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.5
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: Calculate Safe Endurance, Land-By time, and fuel in kg/L
concept: Safe Endurance is defined in the NZ CAA GAP brochure and used throughout the ASPEQ exam bank as: Safe Endurance = usable fuel minus fixed reserve, expressed in time at the cruise burn rate. The Land-By time is computed as Takeoff time + Safe Endurance. Worked example from the GAP brochure: 160 L loaded, 10 L unusable = 150 L usable, fixed reserve 30 min at 40 L/hr = 20 L, so Safe Endurance fuel = 130 L at 40 L/hr = 3 hr 15 min. Takeoff 01:15 UTC gives Land-By = 04:30 UTC. Mass/volume conversions use AvGas SG 0.72 (1 L = 0.72 kg) and Jet A1 SG nominal 0.80 (1 L = 0.80 kg). Always check the question for an exam-specified SG.
exam_tip: Safe Endurance = (usable fuel - fixed reserve) / cruise burn. Land-By time = takeoff + Safe Endurance. Convert L <-> kg with AvGas SG 0.72 and Jet A1 SG 0.80 unless the exam specifies otherwise.
key_facts: Safe Endurance = (usable fuel - fixed reserve) expressed in time at cruise burn rate
Usable fuel = total fuel - unusable fuel (from AFM)
Fixed reserve: 45 min at holding speed 1500 ft AAL for non-turbine (CAR 91.403); 30 min for turbine/helicopter
Land-By time = Takeoff time + Safe Endurance
AvGas: SG ~0.72, so 1 L = 0.72 kg
Jet A1: SG ~0.80, so 1 L = 0.80 kg (exam may specify a precise value)
NZ CAA GAP brochure 'Land-By' worked example is the authoritative format for these calculations
source: CAR 91.403; NZ CAA GAP brochure; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: Determine maximum holding time before diversion (time-based and fuel-based)
concept: The latest time before which the pilot must commence a diversion is found using the more restrictive of the time-based and fuel-based methods. TIME-BASED: Land-By time minus the time required for the diversion (including missed approach/set-heading fuel and approach at the alternate) gives the latest time to leave the holding fix. FUEL-BASED: current fuel minus (fixed reserve at alternate + fuel for the diversion track + approach and missed approach fuel at alternate) gives the fuel available for holding, divided by the hold burn rate gives maximum hold time. Use whichever is SHORTER. Worked example: Land-By 04:07, current time 03:00, diversion track 46 min -> time-based max hold = 67 - 46 = 21 min. Current fuel 68 L, reserve 20 L, diversion 30 L -> fuel-based max hold = (68 - 50) / 40 L/hr = 27 min. Use 21 min (the shorter).
exam_tip: Max holding = min(time-based, fuel-based). Time-based = (Land-By - now) - diversion time. Fuel-based = (current fuel - reserve - diversion fuel - approach/MA fuel) / hold burn rate. Always use the shorter of the two.
key_facts: Time-based max hold = (Land-By - current time) - time required for diversion to alternate
Fuel-based max hold = (current fuel - fixed reserve - diversion fuel - approach/MA fuel) / hold burn
Use whichever is the SHORTER of the two
Include fuel and time for approach at alternate AND any missed approach allowance
Typical missed approach allowance: 6 min / 250 L (turbine example) or 4 min / smaller volume (piston)
Round down the max-hold minutes to stay on the safe side of the reserve
source: CAR 91.403; CAR 91.405; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: Apply INTER and TEMPO fuel allowances to flight plan fuel calculations
concept: When the destination or alternate forecast contains an INTER or TEMPO deterioration that brings conditions below the required minima, additional holding fuel must be carried. The standard NZ convention used in the course and the ASPEQ exam bank is: INTER adds 30 minutes of holding fuel at 1500 ft AAL; TEMPO adds 60 minutes of holding fuel. These allowances are added to the normal alternate/fixed reserve fuel -- they do NOT replace it. The holding burn rate used is the one stated in the AFM / POH at 1500 ft AAL (the same basis as CAR 91.403 fixed reserve). If the forecast has both an INTER and a TEMPO, take the longer allowance (60 min).
exam_tip: INTER in forecast = +30 min holding fuel. TEMPO in forecast = +60 min holding fuel. Added on top of normal reserves, not instead of. Use holding burn rate at 1500 ft AAL.
key_facts: INTER deterioration below minima -> add 30 min holding fuel at 1500 ft AAL
TEMPO deterioration below minima -> add 60 min holding fuel at 1500 ft AAL
Applied in addition to fixed reserve and alternate fuel -- not instead of
Use holding speed / burn rate per AFM at 1500 ft AAL
If both INTER and TEMPO present, use the longer (60 min) allowance
Thunderstorm forecast may require additional holding fuel per operator policy
source: CAR 91.403; CAR 91.405; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: GNSS en route area navigation exemption from MRA/MEA compliance
concept: Per AIP NZ GEN 3.3 5.1.6, where an aircraft has approved en route area navigation equipment (for example, an approved GNSS receiver), compliance with the MRA and MEA published on ground-based-navaid routes is NOT required, provided the RNAV-certified system is being used for navigation. The aircraft must still comply with obstacle clearance floors (MSA, MFA, sector minimums, grid MORA/AMA) and any ATC-assigned altitude. This exemption exists because MRA/MEA are, in the NZ definition, pure signal-reception floors for NDB and VOR airways -- if you are not relying on those ground aids for navigation, the floor does not apply.
exam_tip: GNSS-approved aircraft are exempt from MEA and MRA on ground-based airways (AIP NZ GEN 3.3 5.1.6). Obstacle clearance via MSA / MFA / MORA still applies.
key_facts: AIP NZ GEN 3.3 5.1.6 exempts RNAV-approved aircraft from MRA/MEA compliance on ground-nav routes
Exemption is because MRA/MEA are signal-reception floors in the NZ definition
Obstacle clearance floors (MSA, MFA, MORA/AMA) still apply -- exemption is for the signal floor only
Aircraft must be using the RNAV system for navigation, not just carrying it
Equipment must be approved for en route area navigation (e.g. TSO-C129/C145/C146 GNSS)
source: AIP NZ GEN 3.3 5.1.6; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: Area Minimum Altitude (AMA) / Minimum Off-Route Altitude (MORA) in route selection
concept: AMA (Area Minimum Altitude) and MORA (Minimum Off-Route Altitude) are grid-based minimum altitudes published on some en route charts that guarantee obstacle clearance within a defined grid or within a corridor of the airway. Grid MORA typically guarantees 1000 ft clearance in non-mountainous terrain and 2000 ft in mountainous terrain across the grid square. Route MORA applies within 10 NM of the centreline of an airway. Neither AMA nor MORA is an ATC-enforceable IFR altitude in the same way MEA or MFA is -- they are reference minima for planning, diversion, and emergency use. The NZ enroute chart set does not consistently publish AMA/MORA in the same way as US Jeppesen charts, but the concepts are commonly tested.
exam_tip: AMA/MORA = grid- or corridor-based minimum altitudes for obstacle clearance, used as a planning and emergency reference. 1000 ft clearance non-mountainous, 2000 ft mountainous.
key_facts: AMA: Area Minimum Altitude -- grid square minimum altitude for obstacle clearance
Grid MORA: Minimum Off-Route Altitude covering a grid square (typical 1 deg x 1 deg)
Route MORA: 10 NM either side of the airway centreline
Clearance: 1000 ft above obstacles in non-mountainous, 2000 ft in mountainous terrain
Reference only -- not an ATC-enforced floor like MEA or MFA
Useful for diversion planning, emergency descent, and cross-check against MFA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Annex 4
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: SID performance standards: minimum climb gradient, turn altitude, and category IAS
concept: AIP NZ ENR 1.1 paragraphs 2.2.1-2.2.9 establish the standard SID performance assumptions used in NZ procedure design. Unless the chart states otherwise, every SID assumes the aircraft is capable of a minimum 3.3% climb gradient, equivalent to 200 ft per NM. Turns on the SID are not permitted below 400 ft above the aerodrome level and must be initiated over the runway centreline. Category A/B/C/D IAS for 15 degree bank turns are 120, 165, 265, and 290 kt respectively. The first SID waypoint in NZ is always a flyover waypoint, not a fly-by. Where the procedure requires a higher-than-3.3% gradient, that gradient is stated explicitly on the chart and applies from the DER until the stated altitude; if multiple sectors require a higher gradient, the HIGHEST applies from the DER.
exam_tip: Default SID climb gradient 3.3% (200 ft/NM). No turns below 400 ft AAL, over the centreline. Cat A/B/C/D 15 deg bank speeds 120/165/265/290 kt. First SID waypoint in NZ is always flyover. Higher chart-stated gradient applies from the DER.
key_facts: Default SID minimum climb gradient: 3.3% = 200 ft per NM
No turns on SID below 400 ft above aerodrome elevation
Turns initiated over the runway centreline, not before
15 degree bank IAS limits: Cat A 120 kt, Cat B 165 kt, Cat C 265 kt, Cat D 290 kt
First SID waypoint in NZ is always a flyover (not a fly-by) waypoint
Chart-stated higher gradient applies from DER; if multiple sectors, use the HIGHEST
Climb gradient is the performance floor -- operator and aircraft must validate capability
source: AIP NZ ENR 1.1 2.2.1-2.2.9; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: HSI/CDI deflection scaling: 2-dot vs 5-dot needle interpretation
concept: A Course Deviation Indicator (CDI) on a VOR or HSI display shows lateral displacement from the selected course. Full-scale deflection is always 10 degrees off course (for a VOR in en route mode). The number of dots between centre and full scale varies with the instrument: a 2-dot display has 2 dots per side, so 1 dot = 5 degrees; a 5-dot display has 5 dots per side, so 1 dot = 2 degrees. Exam questions frequently give a dot reading and ask the angular displacement -- check the instrument type first. In localiser mode the CDI sensitivity is higher (typically 2.5 degrees full scale) and the dot-to-degree conversion changes accordingly. RNAV/GNSS CDIs use distance-based deflection (1 NM, 0.3 NM, 0.1 NM full scale depending on mode) rather than degrees.
exam_tip: VOR full scale = 10 deg off course. 2-dot display: 1 dot = 5 deg. 5-dot display: 1 dot = 2 deg. Localiser and RNAV use different sensitivities -- always check the instrument.
key_facts: VOR en route full-scale CDI deflection = 10 degrees
2-dot CDI: 1 dot = 5 degrees
5-dot CDI: 1 dot = 2 degrees
Localiser: typically 2.5 degrees full scale (4x more sensitive than VOR)
RNAV/GNSS CDI: distance-based (1 NM / 0.3 NM / 0.1 NM depending on phase of flight)
Always identify the instrument type before calculating degrees from dots
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Annex 10
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.

syllabus_desc: GNSS substitution restriction for distance step and DME arc procedures
concept: AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.8 restricts the use of GNSS as a substitute for DME when flying a distance step procedure. GNSS may not be used unless the pilot has a direct readout of the distance from the reference point or facility on which the distance steps are based. This is because distance steps are referenced to a specific navaid position and an RNAV cross-track distance or waypoint DTK is not the same thing. The same principle applies to DME arc procedures -- you need a continuous distance indication from the arc centre navaid, not a derived GNSS cross-track. If DME fails during a REVERSE DME STEP departure, the pilot should normally return toward the departure aid, maintain the highest step altitude reached until MSA is confirmed, and advise ATC (AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.7).
exam_tip: AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.8: GNSS may not substitute for DME on a distance step procedure unless the pilot has a direct distance-from-reference readout. DME arc procedures require continuous distance from the arc centre.
key_facts: AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.8: GNSS substitution not permitted on distance step procedures without a direct distance readout
Reason: distance steps are referenced to a specific navaid position, not an RNAV cross-track
DME arcs similarly require continuous DME from the arc centre navaid
If DME fails on a REVERSE DME STEP departure (§8.8.7): return toward the departure aid, maintain the highest step altitude reached, advise ATC
Pilot must verify instrument capability BEFORE accepting a distance step clearance on GNSS alone
source: AIP NZ ENR 1.1 §8.8.7-8.8.8; NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17
audit_note: Added after courseware audit 2026-04-09 to close content gap.
